title: What is the difference between "instance Variable Names" and "class Variable Names", in pharo?
body:
I just created my own package(MyPackage) and class(MyClass) in pharo, using the System Browser. This is how it looks.

Object subclass: #MyClass
    instanceVariableNames: ''
    classVariableNames: ''
    category: 'MyPackage'

I understand the terms subclass and category, not getting the meaning of the other two terms (instanceVariableNames, classVariableNames).
stackoverflow_answer:
I have an impression that this question was already asked and answered, but I was not able to find it, probably it was on a mailing list.

Instance variables are just the variables that are personal to an instance, so each instance of the class that you are defining will have it's own set of variables.

Instance variables of the class side. You can define that when you switch to the class side, then you will se a code like this:

MyClass class
  instanceVariableNames: ''

In Pharo (and Smalltalks in general) each class is an instance of a meta class. The variables can be accessed by class-side methods, and as there is only a single instance representing class object, there will be only one set of these variables of that instance.

Class variables are the variables defined by classVariableNames: '' on the instance side template. I like to call them "pool variables", but in fact if you define such variable, all the instances from the hierarchy will be able to access it. Let's say that you have a class A and it's subclass B. If you have a "class variable" in A, you can access the same variable from both instances of A and B. It's like having a global variable for a hierarchy. I recommend not to use this type of variables.
explanation:
According to the Pharo textbook, "instanceVariableNames" refers to instance variables, which are private to the instance itself. This means that two instances of the same class cannot access each other's instance variables unless the class defines accessor methods. Instance variables can be accessed by name in any of the instance methods of the class that defines them, and also in the methods defined in its subclasses (p. 89).

On the other hand, "classVariableNames" refers to class variables, which are shared between instances and classes. Class variables are similar to static variables in Java and C++, but they are dynamically dispatched, meaning that inheritance, overriding, and super-sends work for class methods in Pharo (p. 92). Class variables are private to the class itself, and instances of the class inherit these variables, but each instance has its own private copy of the variable (p. 92).

In Pharo, instances of the same class do not share instance variables. This is in contrast to Java and C++, where instances of the same class can access each other's instance variables. In Pharo, instances can access their own instance variables, but not those of other instances (p. 89).

Here is a summary:

* Instance variables are private to the instance itself and cannot be accessed by other instances of the same class unless accessor methods are defined.
* Class variables are shared between instances and classes and are dynamically dispatched.
* Class variables are private to the class itself, and instances of the class inherit these variables, but each instance has its own private copy of the variable.

Example:
In the Pharo object model, instance variables are private to the instance itself. For instance, in the Point class, the instance variables x and y are accessed directly by the method Point>>dist:. The instance variables are private to the instance, and other instances of the same class cannot access them directly.

title: Why do methods return "self" by default in Smalltalk?
body:
Background

In Smalltalk, if you don't excplicitly return anything then the message passing evaluates to the receiver (or "self" in the message context).

For example, given this method:

MyClass >> myMethod
  Transcript show: 'hello'; cr.

Evaluating (doint "print-it") this:

| myInstance |
myInstance := MyClass new.
myInstance myMethod.

If <print-it> is done to the last invocation, then the result would be the instance itself.

Questions

  • Why was this designed this way?
  • What is the idea behind it?
  • What was the philosophical background?
  • What are the practical benefits from it? Is it to facilitate method chaining?
stackoverflow_answer:
One very simple reason has not been stated yet: In the Virtual Machine, returning self is simpler and more efficient than returning any other object.

Smalltalk byte codes implement a stack machine. That means arguments are passed by pushing them onto a stack, rather than putting them into registers. In addition to the arguments as listed in the method signature, a hidden argument is always passed, which is the receiver of the message. So even for unary methods (those without arguments) the receiver is pushed onto the stack, then the method is executed, and the receiver value on the stack is how the method knows "self". By returning "self" if no explicit return statement is given, the VM can just leave the "self" oop on the stack, which saves at least one memory store operation. So from an efficiency and simplicity standpoint, returning "self" is the most elegant thing to do.
explanation:
The methods in Smalltalk, including Pharo, return "self" by default. This is because the pseudo-variable "self" represents the receiver of the message, just like the keyword "this" in Java. When a method does not explicitly return a value, it implicitly returns the object that received the message, which is usually referred to as "self" (Pharo, p. 103). This is in contrast to other languages, such as Ruby, which return the value of the last statement in the method.

The reason for returning "self" by default is to emphasize that methods are intended to provide information about the receiver, rather than to return a value of interest to the sender. As Kent Beck notes, "Return a value only when you intend for the sender to use the value" (Pharo, p. 103). This approach encourages a style in which objects tend to have small methods that delegate tasks to other objects, rather than implementing huge, procedural methods that assume too much responsibility (Joseph Pelrine, as quoted in Pharo).

In Pharo, when a method does not explicitly return a value, it answers the object that received the message. This is because everything in Pharo is an object, and objects communicate only by sending each other messages. This approach encourages a style in which objects are designed to be self-contained and do not expose their internal state to other objects (Joseph Pelrine, as quoted in Pharo).

In summary, methods in Smalltalk, including Pharo, return "self" by default because it emphasizes that methods are intended to provide information about the receiver, rather than to return a value of interest to the sender. This approach encourages a style in which objects tend to have small methods that delegate tasks to other objects, rather than implementing huge, procedural methods that assume too much responsibility.

"', "One of the consequences of Pharo 's model of message sending is that it encourages a style in which objects tend to have very small methods and delegate tasks to other objects , rather than implementing huge , procedural methods that assume too much responsibility ."]

title: How do we check in pharo if an element already exist in a set
body:
Say I have a function that continually add a random number to a collection after each refresh.

Myfunction
|list length data |

z := Set new.
x := 8.
data:= received data after each refresh.
length := data size.
1 to: length do:[:k | list := data at: k.
    list < x
    "How to check if this data already exist and not add"
        ifTrue:[z add: list]

How can I check in Pharo if a number already exists and if so it should not add it again?
stackoverflow_answer:
Sets and other basic collections are part of common Smalltalk. You should have a look at the Smalltalk documentation (e.g., the Smalltalk bluebook), particularly the references for each of the data types you are attempting to use as well as other related ones.

If you read the documentation for Set you'll find a message #includes: which will return true if the argument is in the set, and return false if it isn't. That would look like:

(z includes: list) ifTrue: [ z add: list ]

But, a Set by definition contains unique elements. Therefore, if you attempt to add an element that's already in the set, Smalltalk will not add it again and not give you an error if you attempt to do so. So you don't need to check to see if the set already includes list. You can just add it.

Then you are doing your data traversal the long, hard way. You don't need to get the length then loop on an index from 1 to that length. I assume your data is some kind of Smalltalk Collection, so you can just use the #do: message. The #do: message allows you to iterate a block over each element of the collection:

data do: [ :each | each < x ifTrue: [ z add: each ] ]

This replaces everything you have shown starting at length := ....
explanation:
To check if an element already exists in a set in Pharo, you can use the `includes:` message. This message returns `true` if the element is found in the set, and `false` otherwise.

For example, if you have a set `s` and you want to check if the element `x` is already in the set, you can use the following code:
```smalltalk
s includes: x
```
If `x` is already in the set, this message will return `true`. If not, it will return `false`.

In your code, you can modify the `Myfunction` method to check if the element is already in the set before adding it:
```smalltalk
Myfunction
|list length data|
z := Set new.
x := 8.
data:= received data after each refresh.
length := data size.
1 to: length do:[:k | list := data at: k.
    list < x
    "How to check if this data already exist and not add"
        ifTrue:[
            if (z includes: list) then [  "check if the element is already in the set"
                ^self ]  "do nothing if it's already in the set"
            z add: list
        ]
]
```
This code uses the `includes:` message to check if the element is already in the set. If it is, the method returns without adding the element to the set. If not, the element is added to the set.

Note that the `includes:` message is implemented by the `Set` class, so you can use it with any set in Pharo.

title: How to implement a switch in Pharo Smalltalk
body:
I'm trying to parse a command and an int to make a "turtle" move on a board. I'm a bit overwhelmed, since it's not throwing an exception, and I can't even figure out how to open the debugger without one.

My code:

"only should begin parsing on new line"
endsWithNewLine:= aTurtleProgram endsWith: String cr.
endsWithNewLine ifTrue:[
    "splits commands based on new lines"
    commands := aTurtleProgram splitOn: String cr.
    commands do:[:com |
        "should split into command and value for command"
        i := com splitOn: ' '.
        "command"
        bo := ci at: 1.
        val := ci at: 2.
        "value for command"
        valInt := val asInteger.
        ^ bo = 'down' "attempted switch"
            ifTrue: [ turtle down: valInt ]
            ifFalse: [
              bo = 'left'
                  ifTrue: [ turtle left: valInt ]
                  ifFalse: [
                    bo = 'right'
                        ifTrue: [ turtle right: valInt ]
                        ifFalse: [
                          bo = 'up'
                              ifTrue: [ turtle up: valInt ]
                              ifFalse: [ self assert: false ] ] ] ] ].
stackoverflow_answer:
You can do it the way you did it, and you can open a debugger by inserting a self halt statement into your code.

Usually, ifs, and moreover case-sytle ifs, are a bad sign. So what you can do is break the functionality into classes like DownMove, LeftMove and so on, and then each class will implement its own functionality when you call, for example, an execute: method that will do exactly what is needed by the command. But in your case, the approach would be cumbersome; moreover, you have very trivial actions.

You can use a dictionary with definitions. So imagine that you define an instance variable or class variable:

moveActions := {
  'down' -> [ :dist | turtle down: dist ] .
  'left' -> [ :dist | turtle left: dist ] .
  ... } asDictionary

Then in your code, you do: (moveActions at: bo) value: valInt. This snippet will give you a block (value) for a string (key), then you evaluate the block with your integer.

On the other hand, as the action pattern is the same and only message changes, you can map only the message names in the dictionary:

moveActions := {
  'down' -> #down: .
  'left' -> #left: .
  ... } asDictionary

Then you can ask your turtle to perform a message dynamically given by string: 

`turtle perform: (moveActions at: bo) with: valInt`

Also, if you want to rely on the similarity between commands that you read and messages that you send to the turtle, you can dynamically compose the message string:

`turtle perform: (bo, ':') asSymbol with: valInt`

Please note that this is not recommended in your case, as, first of all, you are coupling user input and your code, i.e. if you decide to change the user command from down to moveDown, you will have to change your method name from down: to moveDown:. Also, this approach allows the user to "inject" bad code into your system, as he can write a command like become 42, which will result in the code:

`turtle perform: #become: with: 42`

which will swap pointers between the turtle object and 42. Or you can think about even worse cases. But I hope that this meta excursion was good for you. :)
explanation:
To implement a switch in Pharo Smalltalk, you can override the mouseMove: method to do nothing. However, if you want a more advanced switch-like behavior, you can create a subclass of SimpleSwitchMorph and add the necessary methods.

To create a new class, you can modify the class creation template as follows:

* Replace Object with SimpleSwitchMorph.
* Replace NameOfSubClass with LOCell.
* Add mouseAction to the list of instance variables.

This will create a new class definition that sends a message to the existing class SimpleSwitchMorph, asking it to create a subclass called LOCell. The new class will have a mouseAction instance variable.

To add methods to your class, select the protocol 'no messages' in the protocol pane and replace the template text with the desired method. You can then accept the changes and the class will be compiled.

To implement a switch-like behavior, you can use conditionals and loops. Pharo offers no special syntax for control constructs, but instead, you can express them by sending messages to booleans, numbers, and collections, with blocks as arguments. For example, you can use the ifTrue:, ifFalse: or ifTrue:ifFalse: messages to send a message to a boolean expression.

In your case, you can use a conditional to check the value of the command and then execute the corresponding method. For example:
```
commands do:[:com |
    i := com splitOn: ' '.
    bo := i at: 1.
    val := i at: 2.
    valInt := val asInteger.
    bo = 'down' ifTrue: [turtle down: valInt]
        ifFalse: [
            bo = 'left' ifTrue: [turtle left: valInt]
                ifFalse: [
                    bo = 'right' ifTrue: [turtle right: valInt]
                        ifFalse: [bo = 'up' ifTrue: [turtle up: valInt] ifFalse: [self assert: false]]]]]
```
This code checks the value of the command and then executes the corresponding method (e.g., turtle down: valInt, turtle left: valInt, etc.).

Note that you can also use the debugger to step through your code and see what's happening. To open the debugger, you can use the Do it menu item or press Shift-Option (on OS X) while clicking.

title: In Pharo/Smalltalk: How to read a file with a specific encoding?
body:
I am currently reading a file like this:

dir := FileSystem disk workingDirectory.
stream := (dir / 'test.txt' ) readStream.
line := stream nextLine.

This works when the file is utf-8 encoded but I could not find out what to do when the file has another encoding.
stackoverflow_answer:
For Pharo 7 there's this guide for file streams, which proposes:

('test.txt' asFileReference)
    readStreamEncoded: 'cp-1250' do: [ :stream |
        stream upToEnd ].
explanation:
To read a file with a specific encoding in Pharo/Smalltalk, you can use the `FileStream` class and specify the encoding when creating the file stream. However, the `FileStream` class is character-based by default, which means it will not work correctly if the file contains non-ASCII characters.

To read a file with a specific encoding, you can use the `converter:` message to specify the encoding. For example, to read a file encoded in Latin1, you can use the following code:

```smalltalk
FileStream 
    oldFileOrNoneNamed: path 
    converter: Latin1TextConverter new; 
    contents
```

This code creates a file stream for the file with the given path and specifies the Latin1 encoding. The `contents` message is then used to read the contents of the file.

If the file contains non-ASCII characters, you may need to use a different encoding converter. For example, to read a file encoded in UTF-8, you can use the following code:

```smalltalk
FileStream 
    oldFileOrNoneNamed: path 
    converter: Utf8TextConverter new; 
    contents
```

You can also use the `readStream` message to read the file stream and specify the encoding. For example:

```smalltalk
FileStream 
    oldFileOrNoneNamed: path 
    converter: Latin1TextConverter new; 
    readStream
```

This code creates a read stream for the file with the given path and specifies the Latin1 encoding. The `readStream` message is then used to read the contents of the file.

In Pharo/Smalltalk, there are several ways to create file streams, including:

* `fileNamed:`: Opens a file with the given name for reading and writing.
* `forceNewFileNamed:`: Creates a new file with the given name and opens it for writing.
* `oldFileNamed:`: Opens an existing file with the given name for reading and writing.
* `readOnlyFileNamed:`: Opens an existing file with the given name for reading only.

Each of these messages can be used to create a file stream with the desired encoding.

Reference:
- Pharo By Example, page 223
- Pharo By Example, page 232
- Pharo By Example, page 234

title: Message Not Understood for existing method
body:
Ok, so it's like this:

receiver selectors

returns

#(#expression #expression: #mondrianLabel #accept: #name)

But 

receiver expression

throws MessageNotUnderstood.

Any ides how to fix this and why this can be caused?
stackoverflow_answer:
You are probably sending the message expression to a class instead of an instance of that class.

If the result of evaluating receiver selectors is #(#expression #expression: #mondrianLabel #accept: #name) then receiver is most probably a class. The message #selectors is implemented on the class Behavior and returns the selectors of the instance methods of the class. In other words: instances of the class (or any of its subclasses) understand the messages listed.

Hence, if you evaluate receiver expression, then you will get a message not understood exception because the message #expression is defined on the instance of the class that is the value of your receiver variable.
explanation:
When an existing method does not understand a message, it throws a `MessageNotUnderstood` error. This occurs when the method lookup in the inheritance chain reaches the top of the hierarchy, and the method is not found. According to the Pharo object model (107), the virtual machine causes the object to send itself `doesNotUnderstand:` with the message selector as its argument. This creates a new `MessageNotUnderstood` object that is capable of starting a debugger in the current execution context.

To handle this error, developers can easily override the `doesNotUnderstand:` method in any subclass of Object and provide a different way of handling the error (107). This offers developers an easy way to intercept such errors and take alternative action.

One common application of this technique is to implement lightweight proxies for objects. In this case, a minimal object is introduced to act as a proxy for an existing object. Since the proxy will implement virtually no methods of its own, any message sent to it will be trapped by `doesNotUnderstand:`. By implementing this method, the proxy can then take special action before delegating the message to the real subject it is the proxy for (313).

Another typical use for overriding `doesNotUnderstand:` is to lazily load or compile missing methods (312-313). This can be useful in situations where the method is not yet implemented, but will be in the future.

If `doesNotUnderstand:` is overridden by one of the subclasses of Object in the lookup path, it can be used to realize certain kinds of dynamic behavior. An object that overrides `doesNotUnderstand:` can take alternative action and then forward the message to the original target (312-313).

In Pharo, `doesNotUnderstand:` is sent whenever message lookup fails. The default implementation, i.e., Object>>`doesNotUnderstand:`, will trigger the debugger at this point. It may be useful to override `doesNotUnderstand:` to provide some other behavior (312).

In conclusion, when an existing method does not understand a message, it throws a `MessageNotUnderstood` error. This can be handled by overriding the `doesNotUnderstand:` method in any subclass of Object and providing a different way of handling the error. This technique is commonly used to implement lightweight proxies, lazily load or compile missing methods, and realize dynamic behavior.

title: Invoking shell commands from Squeak or Pharo
body: How can you invoke shell commands from Squeak and Pharo? Do these environments have anything in them like the system() function in certain unix languages to run external shell commands, or the backticks (can't make them here do to the editor, but what you get when you push the key left of "1" and above "TAB") to capture the output of commands?
stackoverflow_answer: In Squeak you can use CommandShell, but I don't know what (if anything) is available for Pharo at this time.
explanation:
To invoke shell commands from Squeak or Pharo, you can follow these steps:

For Linux, assuming you're in the unzipped Pharo5.0 folder, you can use the following command:
```
./pharo shared/Pharo5.0.image
```
For OS X, assuming you're in the directory with the unzipped Pharo5.0.app bundle, you can use the following command:
```
Pharo5.0.app/Contents/MacOS/Pharo
```
Alternatively, you can right-click on Pharo5.0.app and select 'Show Package Contents' to get access to the image. If you need this often, just download a separated image/changes pair and drop that image into the Pharo5.0.app [1].

For Windows, assuming you're in the unzipped Pharo5.0 folder, you can use the following command:
```
Pharo.exe Pharo5.0.image
```
In general, Pharo tries to "do the right thing". If you double-click on the VM, it looks for an image file in the default location. If you double-click on an .image file, it tries to find the nearest VM to launch it with [1].

If you have multiple VMs installed on your machine, the operating system may no longer be able to guess the right one. In this case, it is safer to specify exactly which ones you meant to launch, either by dragging and dropping the image file onto the VM, or specifying the image on the command line [1].

You can also use the Pharo Launcher, a tool that helps you download and manage Pharo images [2]. Additionally, you can use handy scripts like wget to download specific versions of Pharo [3].

References:

[1] Pharo By Example, pages 9-10

[2] Pharo By Example, page 10-11

[3] Pharo By Example, page 7-8

title: How to set day month and year values for a Date or DataAndTime object in Smalltalk (Pharo / Squeak)
body:
I'm trying to find a a pre-defined method for objects of Class Date or Class DateAndTime, that allows me to create an new Date (or a new DateAndTime) by supplying three integers: one integer for the day of the month (1-31); one for the month (1 - 12); and a four-digit integer for the year.

( The closest I've found so far is Integer>>asYear )

Is there a method that can set all three parameters at once?
stackoverflow_answer:
If I understand you correctly, you are trying to create an instance of Date from three integers representing the day, month and year of said date.

When facing a question like this you can browse the class, Date in this case, and check its protocol for instance creation methods. In Pharo there are several methods in this category, but it is easy so see (I think) that #year:month:day looks like a good candidate. So, you can try it. Just evaluate the expression

Date year: 2015 month: 12 day: 31

and see what happens (you can inspect or print it to see the result).

You will also find #newDay:month:year as another good candidate. You could try it too. Or you could just see that it sends our previous message and thus it is just a synonymous (which is present for compatibility with other dialects that support the Smalltalk-80/ANSI specification).
explanation:
According to the Pharo By Example textbook, there is no predefined method for creating a new Date or DateAndTime object by supplying three integers: day of the month, month, and year. However, you can use the asRegex method to convert a date string to a three-element array with year, month, and day strings.

For example, the following code uses a MMM DD, YYYY date format recognizer to convert a date to a three-element array:
```
| date result |
date := '(Jan|Feb|Mar|Apr|May|Jun|Jul|Aug|Sep|Oct|Nov|Dec)\s+(\d\d?)\s*,\s*19(\d\d)' asRegex.
```
As for setting the day, month, and year values for a Date or DateAndTime object, you can use the inspector to examine and modify the object's state. For instance, you can type DateAndTime now in a playground and then right-click and choose Inspect it (CMD-i) or Do it and go (CMD-g) to open an inspector.

Additionally, you can use the `asDate` method to convert a date string to a DateAndTime object, and then use the `translateTo` method to adjust the date as needed. For example:
```
testAsDate
self assert: aDateAndTime asDate = ('February 29, 2004' asDate translateTo: 2 hours).
```
However, there is no single method that allows you to set the day, month, and year values for a Date or DateAndTime object in one step. You may need to use a combination of methods, such as `asDate`, `translateTo`, and the inspector, to achieve your desired outcome.

References:

* "Pharo By Example" textbook, pp. 343-344
* "Pharo By Example" textbook, pp. 130-131
* "Pharo By Example" textbook, pp. 171-172

title: How to sort a Dictionary by values in Smalltalk?
body:
I've got a Dictionary like this:

a PluggableDictionary(
    Rankable1->8.5
    Rankable2->9.0
)

I need just an OrderedCollection with the Rankable objects in descending order:

a OrderedCollection(
    Rankable2
    Rankable1
)

I noticed it is easy to sort by keys, but I found it a bit more difficult to sort by values. What is the smalltalk way of doing this?
stackoverflow_answer:
If you need one shot sorted collection in noncritical loop you might use something like this (uses pharo syntax to initialize example dictionary):

pd := PluggableDictionary newFromPairs: { 'a' . 2 . 'b' . 1 . 'c' . 3} . 

(pd associations asSortedCollection: [:x :y | x value < y value]) 
            collect: [:assoc | assoc key].

If you would need it more often, than you might consider introducing your own class that will keep this collection calculated.
explanation:
To sort a Dictionary by values in Smalltalk, you can iterate over the values of the dictionary and create an OrderedCollection with the Rankable objects in descending order. 

Here's how you can do it:

```smalltalk
| dictionary |
dictionary := PluggableDictionary new.
dictionary at: Rankable1 put: 8.5.
dictionary at: Rankable2 put: 9.0.

| orderedCollection |
orderedCollection := dictionary values.
orderedCollection := orderedCollection asSortedCollection.
orderedCollection := orderedCollection collect: [ :rankable | rankable ].

 Transcript show: orderedCollection; cr.
```

In this code, we first create a PluggableDictionary and add the Rankable objects and their corresponding values. Then, we iterate over the values of the dictionary, sort them in descending order, and collect the Rankable objects in an OrderedCollection.

The Transcript statement at the end is used to print the ordered collection.

Note that this code assumes that the values in the dictionary are comparable and that the Rankable objects support the `>` and `<` messages for comparison.

title: How to check if two arrays contain the same elements?
body:
Is there any simple way to check if two arrays contain the same elements?

Here is my try were I return true if arrays are not the same:

arr1 := #(1 3 5 6).
arr2 := #(1 2 3 4).
arr2Copy := arr2 copyFrom: 1 to: arr2 size.
arr1 size ~= arr2 size
    ifTrue: [^ true].
arr1
    do: [:a | (arr2copy removeFirst = a)
        ifFalse: [^ true]].
^false
stackoverflow_answer:
If the elements should be equal and in the same order, you can just compare the arrays with =.

If the elements should be equal and the order does not matter and there are no duplicates to be expected, use array1 asSet = arr2 asSet.

Otherwise you can check out hasEqualElements:, and asBag as well.

If the elements should be identical and in the same order, how about this?

array1 with: array2 do:
    [ :a :b | a == b ifFalse: [ ^ false ]].
 ^ true

It iterates over the two arrays simultaneously, comparing the identities of elements at the same indices. If any are not identical, return false. If no distinct elements were encountered, return true.
explanation:
To check if two arrays contain the same elements, you can't simply use the `=` operator because it checks for object identity, not for equality of elements. You can, however, use the `asSet` method to convert the arrays to sets, and then check if the sets are equal.

Here's an example:
```smalltalk
arr1 := #(1 3 5 6).
arr2 := #(1 2 3 4).

arr1 asSet = arr2 asSet
```
This will return `true` if the arrays contain the same elements, and `false` otherwise.

Alternatively, you can use the `withAll:` method to create a new collection that contains all the elements from both arrays, and then check if the collection is equal to itself:
```smalltalk
arr1 := #(1 3 5 6).
arr2 := #(1 2 3 4).

OrderedCollection withAll: arr1 withAll: arr2
```
This will return the same result as the first example.

It's worth noting that the `=` operator is not sufficient to check for equality of arrays, because it checks for object identity, not for equality of elements. For example:
```smalltalk
arr1 := #(1 2 3).
arr2 := arr1 copy.

arr1 = arr2
```
This will return `true`, even though the arrays contain different elements. This is because the arrays are the same object, but the elements are different.

To check for equality of arrays, you need to use a different method, such as the `asSet` method or the `withAll:` method.

Reference:
* Pharo By Example, page 182: "The default implementation of object equality is to test for object identity..."
* Pharo By Example, page 218: "Suppose that we want two books to be considered equal if their titles and authors are the same. Then we would redefine not only = but also hash as follows..."

title: Squeak or Pharo for the beginning Smalltalker?
body:
I am a Rubyist that is just starting to dabble in Smalltalk and am trying to chose an implementation to experiment with. It seems like Squeak or Pharo would be a good choice, but I'm having trouble finding current information to help decide which one would be better. Much of what I find via Google searches is from a couple of years ago and may no longer be relevant.

Is there a clear favorite these days? If not, what are the pros and cons of each?
stackoverflow_answer:
To certain extent, it is a matter of personal taste. Pharo is fork of Squeak intended make it more modern version of Smalltalk, sometimes at expense of not sticking to backward compatibility to Squeak.

Pharo being a fork, also means that they share much in common, from VM to huge parts of class library. Many add on packages work with both of them, and some are developed with Squeak as primary dev platform and I guess a slightly larger number of them are developed in Pharo (Seaside, Pier, etc). Both variants have free intro books: Squeak by Example, and Pharo by Example, but Pharo one seems more up to date.

Personally I prefer Pharo since it gives me cleaner and more modern feeling.
explanation:
The question is whether to choose Squeak or Pharo as the first Smalltalk implementation to experiment with. Since you are a Rubyist, it is likely that you are interested in exploring the Smalltalk family of languages.

Squeak is a modern implementation of Smalltalk-80, while Pharo is a fork of Squeak. Both are open-source and offer a lot of flexibility in terms of development. Pharo is highly portable and can run on various platforms, including OS X, Windows, Linux, Android, and iOS. It is also the reference implementation for the Seaside web development framework, which is a popular framework for building web applications.

Pharo is a modern, open-source, dynamically typed language that supports live coding inspired by Smalltalk. It has a simple syntax that can be easily learned, and it offers a powerful IDE, a rich library, and a culture that encourages and values changes and improvements.

Pharo strives to offer a lean, open platform for professional software development, as well as a robust and stable platform for research and development into dynamic languages and environments. It is a great choice for developers who are interested in exploring the Smalltalk family of languages and are looking for a platform that is highly portable and offers a lot of flexibility in terms of development.

In terms of getting started with Pharo, it is recommended to download the Pharo One-Click Experience, which is a prepackaged version of the latest stable version of Pharo for your operating system. This will give you a Pharo image that is ready to use, and you can start exploring the language and its ecosystem.

In terms of what to do to start Pharo, you can simply download the Pharo One-Click Experience and launch it. For Linux and Mac OS X, you can double-click on the Pharo image file to launch it. For Windows, you can enter the unzipped Pharo folder and double-click on the Pharo.exe file.

In terms of what Pharo is an example of, it is an example of a modern, open-source, dynamically typed language that supports live coding inspired by Smalltalk. It has a simple syntax that can be easily learned, and it offers a powerful IDE, a rich library, and a culture that encourages and values changes and improvements.

In terms of what Pharo is used for, it is used for a wide range of innovative projects, from multimedia applications and educational platforms to commercial web development environments. It is a great choice for developers who are interested in exploring the Smalltalk family of languages and are looking for a platform that is highly portable and offers a lot of flexibility in terms of development.

title: Smalltalk - Is it possible to add a string to a String instance via a method?
body:
Smalltalk - Is it possible to add a string to a String instance via a method?

Essentially I'd like something along the lines of:

renderThisOn: aString

aString append: 'whatever text I want'

Essentially I'd like a String instance (ByteString, etc) to behave like the "html" object in Seaside. I pass it on as an argument to multiple methods, each adding some information to it.
stackoverflow_answer:
From a practical viewpoint the answer would be no, it is not possible to change the size of a String. You can modify the characters of the String though:

a := 'abc'.
a at: 2 put: $x.
a = 'axc' "true"

Therefore, when you concatenate two strings you get a third one, while the others two remain unchanged

s := 'abc'.
t := 'def'.
u := s , t.
s = 'abc'. "true"
t = 'def'. "true"

Having said this, there is actually a way to do grow (or shrink) a String. The idea is to use become: (or becomeForward:). This message will replace all references to the receiver with references to the argument. In your case:

s := 'abc'.
t := 'def'.
assoc := s -> 3 "referene to s"
s become: s , t.
s = 'abcdef'. "true"
assoc key == s "true"

The reason why I started my answer by saying that you cannot change the string's size is because in the vast majority of cases the use of become: is overkilling and the recommended practice is to review the code and eliminate the need for modifying the structure of an object.
explanation:
To define a global in Smalltalk, you can provide class methods to access them. In Pharo, all global variables are stored in a namespace called Smalltalk, which is implemented as an instance of the class SystemDictionary. Global variables are accessible everywhere. Every class is named by a global variable. In addition, a few globals are used to name special or commonly useful objects.

To define a String in Pharo, you can use the following syntax: a String is a collection of Characters. It is sequenceable, indexable, mutable and homogeneous, containing only Character instances. Like Arrays, Strings have a dedicated syntax, and are normally created by directly specifying a String literal within single quotes, but the usual collection creation methods will work as well.

To modify a String using replaceAll:with:, you can use the following syntax: s replaceAll: $n with: $N. s replaceFrom: 4 to: 5 with: 'wo'. s >>> 'No worries'.

To create a SortedCollection in Pharo, you can use the following syntax: SortedCollection new add: 5; add: 2; add: 50; add: -10; yourself. You can also convert an existing collection to a SortedCollection by sending it the message asSortedCollection.

To get a String back from a SortedCollection, you can use the following syntax: 'hello' asSortedCollection as: String. or String newFrom: 'hello' asSortedCollection. or String withAll: 'hello' asSortedCollection.

In Pharo, you can use the following methods to convert a collection to another kind of collection: asArray, asOrderedCollection, asSortedCollection, asSet, asBag, asDictionary, etc.

The syntax for creating a SortedCollection is: SortedCollection new. You can add elements to it using the add: message. You can also convert an existing collection to a SortedCollection by sending it the message asSortedCollection.

The syntax for creating a String is: 'Hello'. You can also create a String using the newFrom: message: 'Hello' asSortedCollection.

You can modify a String using the replaceAll:with: message. You can also use the replaceFrom:to:with: message to replace a portion of the string.

You can access the elements of a set using iterators.

title: Check if an object is an instance of a given class or of a subclass of it
body:
Is there an easy way to do this in Smalltalk? I'm 80% sure that there is some method but can't find it anywhere.

I know that I can use

(instance class = SomeClass) ifTrue:

And I know that I can use superclass etc... but I hope that there is something built in :)
stackoverflow_answer:
To test whether anObject is instance of aClass:

(anObject isMemberOf: aClass)

To test whether it is an instance of aClass or one of it subclasses:

(anObject isKindOf: aClass)
explanation:
According to the provided text chunks, the methods that allow querying the class of an object in Pharo are:

* `class`: You can ask any object about its class using the message `class`.
* `isMemberOf`: You can ask if an object is an instance of a specific class using the message `isMemberOf:`.
* `isKindOf`: Object>>`isKindOf:` answers whether the receiver's class is either the same as, or a subclass of the argument class.

These methods can be used to check if an object is an instance of a given class or of a subclass of it. For example, you can use `1 isKindOf: SmallInteger` to check if the object `1` is an instance of `SmallInteger` or one of its subclasses.

title: How to print an integer to transcript
body:
I've tried the code

|myNum|
myNum := SmallInteger new: 0.
Transcript show: (myNum printString).

, but Pharo crashes upon running this code.
stackoverflow_answer:
You can't create a SmallInteger using new:. You already have the number you want to print. It's already a SmallInteger. Just use it like such:

Transcript show: 0 printString
explanation:
The Transcript is an object that is often used for logging system messages. It is a kind of system console. In Pharo, you can print an integer to the Transcript by using the `Transcript show:` message. For example:

```smalltalk
|myNum|
myNum := SmallInteger new: 0.
Transcript show: (myNum printString).
```

However, Pharo crashes upon running this code. This is because `Transcript show:` expects a string as its argument, but `myNum printString` returns an integer. To fix this, you can use the `Transcript show:` message with a string argument. For example:

```smalltalk
Transcript show: 'The answer is: ', (3 + 4) printString.
```

This code will print the string "The answer is: 7" to the Transcript.

In Pharo, integers are truly objects. You can send messages to them, just as you do to any other object. For example:

```smalltalk
3 + 4
>>> 7
```

This code will evaluate the expression `3 + 4` and print the result `7` to the Transcript. You can also use the `Transcript show:` message to print the result of an expression. For example:

```smalltalk
Transcript show: (3 + 4) printString.
```

This code will print the string "7" to the Transcript.

title: Regex: Matching all words in a phrase
body:
Is this possible?

For a sentence such as hello how are you, I'd like my regular expression to return hello how are you. It only ever returns just hello and not the other words.

My Regex: 

[A-Za-z]*

Any help is greatly appreciated. Thanks! If it matters, I'm using Pharo Smalltalk. I've been testing in c# too.
stackoverflow_answer:
If you only need split the sentence by spaces this can be done using string.Split() method:

var s = "hello how are you";
var words = s.Split();

If you want to use regular expressions:

var s = "hello how are you";
var regex = "\\w+";
var words = Regex.Matches(s, regex).Cast<Match>().Select(m => m.Value);
explanation:
Based on the provided text chunks, the significance of 'word boundary' in the example regex is that it matches the empty string at a word boundary. This is shown in the example:

'hello world' matchesRegex: '.*\bw.*'
>>> true
"word boundary before w"

This means that the regular expression will match the string 'hello' as a whole word, rather than as part of another word.

As for what a set matches in regex, a set matches any single character if it appears between the brackets. For example:

[01] matches either 0 or 1

Using a set, you can build a binary number recognizer:

'10010100' matchesRegex: '[01]+'
>>> true

A set may also include ranges: pairs of characters separated by a hyphen (-). This is equivalent to listing all characters in between:

'[0-9]' is the same as '[0123456789]'

Finally, allRegexMatches returns a collection of all matches (substrings of the receiver string) of the regular expression. For example:

'allRegexMatches: returns a collection of all matches (substrings of the receiver string) of the regular expression.
'Jack and Jill went up the hill' allRegexMatches: '\w+'
>>> an OrderedCollection('Jack' 'and' 'Jill' 'went' 'up' 'the' 'hill')

title: What is the difference between self and yourself in Smalltalk?
body:
In Smalltalk, there are two terms often found within a method body: self and yourself.

What is the difference between them?
stackoverflow_answer:
The reserved word self is a pseudo variable (you cannot assign to it) that refers to the current receiver of the method where it is used. On the other side yourself is a message you can send to any object to get that very same object.

The implementation of yourself is

yourself
    ^self

meaning that the message yourself will behave as I just explained.

The reason why yourself exists is to support message cascading, where you put it as the last message to make sure the resulting expression will answer with the receiver:

^receiver
   msg1;
   msg2;
   yourself

If msg2 might answer with something different from the receiver you can append the yourself message to ignore that answer and return receiver instead. Of course you could have achieved the same result by writing:

receiver
   msg1;
   msg2.
^receiver

Because of the simplicity of these two examples, it might be hard to understand what the advantage would be. However, consider that receiver is not a variable but a complex expression, something like.

^(self msg: arg1 arg: arg2)
   msg1;
   msg2;
   yourself.

Without using yourself you would have to add a temporary to save the value of the receiver to achieve the same:

| answer |
answer := self msg: arg1 arg: arg2.
answer
   msg1;
   msg2.
^answer

which is a little bit more verbose.

To summarize, self is a reserved word that refers to the current receiver and yourself is just a regular method that is there just for convenience.
explanation:
The difference between self and yourself in Smalltalk is that self represents the receiver of the message, while yourself is not a valid Smalltalk term.

According to the Pharo by Example textbook, self is a pseudo-variable that represents the receiver of the message and the lookup of the method starts in the class of the receiver (p. 103). This means that when you send a message to self, the method lookup starts in the class of the receiver, and the method is looked up by starting in the receiver's class.

On the other hand, super represents the receiver of the message, but the method lookup starts in the superclass of the class of the method where the super send occurs (p. 105). This means that when you send a message to super, the method lookup starts in the superclass of the class, and the method is looked up by starting in the superclass.

In summary, self represents the receiver of the message and the method lookup starts in the class of the receiver, while super represents the receiver of the message and the method lookup starts in the superclass of the class of the method where the super send occurs.

title: Smalltalk initialization variables
body:
In languages like Java and C++ we give parameters to constructors.

How do you do this in Pharo Smalltalk?

I want something like 

|aColor|
aColor = Color new 'red'.

Or is this bad practice and should I always do

|aColor|
aColor = Color new.
aColor name:= red.d
stackoverflow_answer:
The short answer is that you can do pretty much the same in Smalltalk. From the calling code it would look like:

aColor := Color named: 'Red'.

The long answer is that in Smalltalk you don't have constructors, at least not in the sense that you have a special message named after the class. What you do in Smalltalk is defining class-side messages (i.e. messages understood by the class, not the instance[*]) where you can instantiate and configure your instances. Assuming that your Color class has a name instance variable and a setter for it, the #named: method would be implemented like:

(class) Color>>named: aName
| color |
color := self new.
color name: aName.
^color.

Some things to note:

  • We are using the #new message sent to the class to create a new instance. You can think of the #new message as the primitive way for creating objects (hint: you can browse the implementors of the #new message to see how it is implemented).
  • We can define as many class methods as we want to create new 'configured' instances (e.g. Color fromHexa:) or return pre-created ones (e.g. Color blue).
  • You can still create an uninitialized instance by doing Color new. If you want to forbid that behavior then you must override the #new class message.

There are many good books that you can read about Smalltalk basics at Stef's Free Online Smalltalk Books

[*] This is quite natural due to the orthogonal nature on Smalltalk, since everything (including classes) is an object. If you are interested check Chapter 13 of Pharo by Example or any other reference to classes and metaclasses in Smalltalk.

HTH
explanation:
**Instance Variables in Pharo**

In Pharo, instance variables are variables that are private to the instance itself. They are not shared between instances of the same class. Each instance of a class has its own private set of instance variables. Instance variables can be accessed by name in any of the instance methods of the class that defines them, and also in the methods defined in its subclasses. This is in contrast to Java and C++, where instance variables are shared between instances of the same class.

**Class Variables in Pharo**

Class variables in Pharo are shared between instances and classes. They are not shared with subclasses. Class variables are used to share data between instances and classes. They are accessed using class methods. Pharo provides three kinds of shared variables: globally shared variables, class variables, and pool variables.

**Initializing Class Variables**

Class variables can be initialized using the `initialize` method. This method is called automatically when the class is initialized. It can also be called explicitly using the `initialize` message.

**Global Variables in Pharo**

Global variables in Pharo are stored in a namespace called Smalltalk, which is implemented as an instance of the class SystemDictionary. Global variables are accessible everywhere. They are used to name special or commonly useful objects.

**Defining a Global in Smalltalk**

To define a global in Smalltalk, you can simply perform Do it on an assignment to a capitalized but undeclared identifier. The parser will then offer to declare the global for you. Alternatively, you can use the `Smalltalk globals at: #AGlobalName put: nil` message to define a global programmatically.

**Accessing a Global Object in Smalltalk**

To access a global object in Smalltalk, you can use the `Smalltalk globals at: #AGlobalName` message. For example, `Smalltalk globals at: #Boolean` will return the Boolean class.

**Best Practice for Using Globals**

The recommended practice is to strictly limit the use of global variables. It is usually better to use class instance variables or class variables, and to provide class methods to access them.

title: For loop for array in Pharo Smalltalk
body:
I'm trying to make an array with random numbers (just 0 or 1), but when I run it, it just prints this: End of statement list encountered ->

This is my code:

GenList
 | lista |
  lista := Array new: 31.
  1 to: 30 do: [ :i | lista at: i put: 2 atRandom - 1]
  ^lista

What can I do?
stackoverflow_answer:
Some interesting things to consider:

1. The method selector doesn't start with a lowercase letter

It is a tradition for selectors to start with a lowercase letter. In this sense, genLista would be more correct than GenLista.

2. The method selector includes the abbreviated word 'gen'

For instance, genLista could be renamed to genereLista o listaAlAzar (if you decide to use Spanish)

3. The Array named lista has 31 elements, not 30

The result of Array new: 31 is an array of 31 elements. However, the code below it only fills 30 of them, leaving the last one uninitialized (i.e., nil). Possible solution: lista := Array new: 30.

4. A dot is missing causing a compilation error

The code

  1 to: 30 do: [ :i | lista at: i put: 2 atRandom - 1]
  ^lista

does not compile because there is no dot indicating the separation between the two sentences. Note that the error happens at compilation time (i.e., when you save the method) because the return token ^ must start a statement (i.e., it cannot be inlined inside a statement).

There are other cases where a missing dot will not prevent the code from compiling. Instead, an error will happen at runtime. Here is a (typical) example:

1 to: 10 do: [:i | self somethingWith: i]     "<- missing dot here"
self somethingElse

the missing dot will generate the runtime error self not understood by block.

5. There is a more expressive way of generating 0s and 1s at random

The calculation 2 atRandom - 1 is ok. However, it forces the reader to mentally do the math. A better way to reveal your intention would have been

#(0 1) atRandom

6. When playing with random numbers don't forget to save the seed

While it is ok to use atRandom, such a practice should only be used with "toy" code. If you are developing a system or a library, the recommended practice is to save the seed somewhere before generating any random data. This will allow you to reproduce the generation of random quantities later on for the sake of debugging or confirmation. (Note however, that this will not suffice for making your program deterministically reproducible because unordered (e.g. hashed) collections could form differently in successive executions.)
explanation:
Here are the answers to your questions based on the provided text chunks:

**What are the most generic ways to create instances of collections in Smalltalk?**

The most generic ways to create instances of collections in Smalltalk are:

* `new: anInteger` creates a collection of size `anInteger` whose elements will all be `nil`.
* `with: anObject` creates a collection and adds `anObject` to the created collection.
* `with:with:`, `with:with:with:`, `with:with:with:with:`, and `withAll:` can be used to create collections with initial elements.

**What is "OrderedCollection withAll" in Pharo?**

"OrderedCollection withAll" is a message that adds all elements of one kind of collection to another kind of collection. For example, `OrderedCollection withAll: #(4 5 6 1 2 3)` adds all the elements of the array `(4 5 6 1 2 3)` to an `OrderedCollection`.

**What methods are used to access and put elements in Pharo's arrays?**

The methods used to access and put elements in Pharo's arrays are:

* `at: anInteger` returns the element at index `anInteger`.
* `at: anInteger put: anObject` puts `anObject` at index `anInteger`.
* `add: anObject` adds `anObject` to the end of the array.
* `addAll: aCollection` adds all elements of `aCollection` to the array.

**How do you create a dynamic array in Pharo?**

You can create a dynamic array in Pharo using the `{ ... }` syntax. For example, `{ 1 + 2 }` creates a dynamic array containing the result of the expression `1 + 2`.

**How do you convert a collection to another kind of collection in Pharo?**

You can convert a collection to another kind of collection in Pharo using messages such as `asArray`, `asOrderedCollection`, `asSortedCollection`, `asSet`, `asBag`, etc. For example, `(1 to: 5) asOrderedCollection` converts an `Interval` to an `OrderedCollection`.

**What is the difference between `Array` and `OrderedCollection` in Pharo?**

`Array` and `OrderedCollection` are both sequenceable collections, but they have different semantics. `Array` is a fixed-size collection that can be accessed by integer indices, while `OrderedCollection` is a dynamic collection that can be accessed by integer indices and can be added to or removed from.

title: How to access class variables in Smalltalk
body:
I am trying to access a class variable in one of my classes in Smalltalk.

I have two classes: Class1 and Class2.

Class1 has the following variables: year month day hour minute. Class2 has the following variables: start-time end-time. In the initialize method for Class2 I have the following:

start-time := Class1 new.
end-time := Class1 new.

Now I want to assign the year 2012 to start-time, how do I access the year variable in the Class1 object start-time?
stackoverflow_answer:
Since you are sending new message to the classes I will assume that you are interested in instance variables, and not class variables (shared variables) (see Pharo Object Model in Updated Pharo By Example to understand the differences).

In Pharo all class/instance variables are private, thus the way to access them is to create accessors.

Add to your Class1 methods

Class1>>year
    ^ year

Class1>>year: aYear
    year := aYear

And then you can send the message to the class with the appropriate value:

Class2>>initialize
    startTime := Class1 new.
    startTime year: 2012.

    "or by using a cascade"
    startTime := Class1 new
        year: 2012;
        yourself.

If for whatever reason you needed to access a variable without accessors, you can use metaprogramming:

startTime instVarNamed: #year "meta-getter"
startTime instVarNamed: #year put: 2012 "meta-setter"

Finally, 'start-time' is not a valid variable name.
explanation:
To access class variables in Smalltalk, you can use the `Smalltalk globals` namespace. Class variables are variables that are shared between all instances of a class and the class itself. They are accessed using the `at:` method, for example:

```
Smalltalk globals at: #SomeGlobalVariable
```

This will return the value of the global variable `SomeGlobalVariable`. You can also define a global programmatically using the `Smalltalk globals at: put:` method:

```
Smalltalk globals at: #SomeGlobalVariable put: nil
```

This will create a new global variable named `SomeGlobalVariable` and set its value to `nil`.

In Pharo, class variables are similar to global variables, but they are shared between all instances of a class and the class itself. You can access class variables using the `at:` method, for example:

```
ClassVariableName class >> at: #SomeKey
```

This will return the value of the class variable `SomeKey` for the class `ClassVariableName`.

It's worth noting that class variables are not the same as global variables, and they are not accessible from outside the class. Additionally, class variables are not inherited by subclasses, so each class has its own separate set of class variables.

In general, it's recommended to avoid using global variables and instead use class variables or instance variables to encapsulate data. This makes it easier to manage and maintain your code.

title: Pharo method with multiple arguments
body:
I am creating a Pharo Class method that takes 3 arguments. I am using the following code and it gives me the error "Variable or expression expected.."

    MethodName: arg1:argValue1 arg2:argValue2
    ^ self var1: argValue1 var2: self var3: argValue2

What would be the correct method declaration syntax? Later on, I intend on calling this method like below :

    ClassName var1: argValue1 var2: self var3: argValue2
stackoverflow_answer:
The bit that you have to understand when it comes to naming methods in Smalltalk is that the method can be split into multiple parts, delimited by colons (:), and that the arguments are inserted after each of those colons. Semantically, that makes a lot of sense, and allows you to use good naming practices so that your code reads almost like an English sentence (which is why the language was named Smalltalk).

So for a method that, in Java or a similar "curly bracket language", might look something like this:

registerUser(String emailAddress, String password, boolean isAdmin) {...}

you would split up the method name in the declaration to fit the arguments, like this:

registerUserWithEmail: anEmailAddress password: aPassword isAdmin: aBoolean

making the method name (often prefixed with # in Smalltalk because it is registered as a "symbol" in a global dictionary):

#registerUserWithEmail:password:isAdmin:

That whole thing is the method name, and when calling it, you'd insert the appropriate arguments after the colons (let's assume this method is defined in the class UserRegistry):

UserRegistry
    registerUserWithEmail: 'joe@bloggs.com'
    password: 'topSecret'
    isAdmin: true

Of course you can leave all that on one line, depending on its length and your readability preferences.

Getting back to your case, you had this:

MethodName: arg1:argValue1 arg2:argValue2

The problem to which your compiler is trying to alert you when it says "Variable or expression expected" is that there's nothing between MethodName: and arg1:. I think you may have assumed that MethodName: wasn't part of the method name but part of the definition.

Some notes:

  • by convention, method names use lowerCamelCase
  • #methodName:arg1:arg2: wouldn't make a very good method name in Smalltalk, because the name should describe what it does and, when arguments come into play, what arguments are expected; the same goes for #var1:var2:var3:
  • if you're going to be calling this method by sending a message to ClassName, then that method needs to be defined on the class side of ClassName, not on the instance side
  • think about what you're calling/passing in your example - unless you're doing something complicated with a hierarchy of objects, there may not be much point in sending a message to self with one of the arguments being self as well; ask yourself whether you can simplify something there (hard to be more concrete without knowing what you're trying to do)
explanation:
I've extracted the relevant text chunks and combined them to provide a detailed explanation of Pharo's method with multiple arguments.

**Pharo method with multiple arguments**

In Pharo, you can create a method that takes multiple arguments. The method declaration syntax is as follows:

`MethodName: arg1:argValue1 arg2:argValue2 ...`

For example:

`myMethod: x:5 y:3`

This method takes two arguments `x` and `y` with values `5` and `3`, respectively.

When calling this method, you would use the same syntax:

`myClass myMethod: x:5 y:3`

This is equivalent to sending a message to an object, and the receiver of the message will decide which method to execute.

**Method syntax**

Pharo's syntax is minimal and focuses on sending messages. Expressions are built up from a small number of primitive elements, such as message sends, assignments, closures, and returns.

**Method lookup**

Method lookup in Pharo follows the inheritance chain. The receiver of the message will decide which method to execute, and the lookup starts in the class of the receiver. If the method is not found, the lookup will continue in the superclass of the class.

**Message structure**

There are three types of messages in Pharo: unary, binary, and keyword messages. The order in which messages are sent is determined by the type of message. Unary messages are sent first, followed by binary messages, and then keyword messages. Parentheses can be used to change the order of execution.

**Everything happens by sending messages**

This rule captures the essence of programming in Pharo. Instead of invoking methods, you send messages to objects. The receiver of the message will decide which method to execute, and the method lookup will follow the inheritance chain.

I hope this helps! Let me know if you have any further questions.

title: making a constructor in Pharo or Smalltalk
body:
I want to implement a small class in Pharo so I have done this:

Object subclass: #Person
    instanceVariableNames: 'name age'
    classVariableNames: ''
    category: 'Test'

and I want to simulate a constructor so I have made a method like the following:

newName:aName newAge:aAge
    "comment stating purpose of message"

    name:=aName.
    age:=aAge.
    ^self.

but when I want to call it from the playground in Pharo like this:

objPerson:=Person newName:'Carl' newAge: 10.

it is not recognized by Pharo, what am I doing wrong?
stackoverflow_answer:
The expression Person newName: 'Carl' newAge: 10 is a message to the class object Person. Therefore you have to implement it on the class side of Person.

Your code needs to be tweaked like this

Person class >> newName: aName newAge: anAge
  | person |
  person := self new.
  person name: aName.
  person age: anAge.
  ^person

Note that in the code above self refers to the class because the method is on the class side. But since person is an instance of Person, the messages name: aName and age: anAge have to be defined on the instance side.

Therefore, on the instance side, you need to add the two setters:

Person >> name: aString
  name := aString

Person >> age: anInteger
  age := anInteger

With these three methods you should be able to run your example.

Some additional comments on the coding style:

Firstly, I would have chosen a different selector for the "constructor" method (in Smalltalk we call these "instance creation" methods). For instance,

Person class >> named: aString age: anInteger
  ^self new name: aString; age: anInteger

Secondly, there is no need to use the temporary person for the newly created instance as the expression self new already refers to such an instance.

Finally, note the use of the cascade syntax in

^self new name: aString; age: anInteger

which means that the message age: anInteger will be sent to the same receiver that name: aString, which in this case happens to be the new instance returned by self new.

title: SmallTalk - Printing contents of an OrderedCollection object using Transcript
body:
|oc|
oc := OrderedCollection new. 
oc add: 2.
oc add: #(4 9).
oc Transcript show: self; cr.

Upon running the following code in Pharo, I am getting the message:

MessageNotUnderstood: OrderedCollection>> Transcript

When replacing 'self' with 'oc' I am still getting the same error. I am looking for a way to output the collection using the Transcript.

Why can't Transcript be the receiver of my code?
stackoverflow_answer:
Remember the object message: parameter syntax: you're trying to send the Transcript message to the oc object, and then send the show: message to the object returned by that, with the self parameter.

What you really want to do is ask the Transcript object to show: your oc object. So, do that: send Transcript the show: message with oc as a parameter: Transcript show: oc. That will show the string representation of the collection.

If you were to print each member of the collection (instead of printing the collection itself), you should use the do: method to iterate over them: oc do: [ :element | Transcript show: element ]. Here you print each of the collection's member string representation.

oc := OrderedCollection new. 
oc add: 2.
oc add: #(4 9).
Transcript show: 'Show the collection:'; cr.
Transcript show: oc; cr.
Transcript show: 'Show each element:'; cr.
oc do: [ :element | Transcript show: element; cr ].

Gives this output:

Show the collection:
an OrderedCollection(2 #(4 9))
Show each element:
2
#(4 9)
explanation:
The Transcript is an object that is often used for logging system messages. However, it is not possible to send a message directly to the Transcript from an OrderedCollection object, as the Transcript does not understand the OrderedCollection class.

The reason for this is that the Transcript understands the messages that are understood by its superclass, Collection. Since OrderedCollection is a subclass of Collection, the Transcript does not understand the OrderedCollection class.

To print the contents of an OrderedCollection object using the Transcript, you can convert the OrderedCollection to a collection that the Transcript understands, such as an Array or a SortedCollection. For example:
```
| oc |
oc := OrderedCollection new.
oc add: 2.
oc add: #(4 9).
oc asArray Transcript show: oc; cr.
```
This will print the contents of the OrderedCollection to the Transcript.

Alternatively, you can use a WriteStream to print the contents of the OrderedCollection. For example:
```
| oc |
oc := OrderedCollection new.
oc add: 2.
oc add: #(4 9).
String streamContents: [ :stream |
    stream
        nextPutAll: 'OrderedCollection: ';
        print: oc;
        cr
].
```
This will print the contents of the OrderedCollection to a string, which can then be displayed in the Transcript.

It's also important to note that the `show:` message is not understood by the Transcript, it's a message understood by its superclass, Collection.

title: How do we write if elseif elseif in pharo?
body:
I have to check 3 conditons using the if elseif elseif. How can I do that in pharo. I did but am not sure since I did not find any such application in pharo.

x = 25
ifTrue:[DoSomething]
ifFalse:[DoSomething else].

x < 25
ifTrue: [DoSomething]
ifFalse:[Domething else].

x > 25
ifTrue: [DoSomething ]
ifFalse:[DoSomething else].
stackoverflow_answer:
You can choose different design (using polymorphism, lookup, ...) but that is pretty much the same for any OO language (for Smalltalk in particular, refer to this Refactoring if-chains in Smalltalk without class explosion ).

In Smalltalk (and couple other languages such as Ruby) you have one extra option and that is class extension. You can design your own "if" statements that match well your particular domain and makes the code more obvious.

E.g. in your given example, I could add a new method to the Number class called compareTo:lesser:equal:greater:, and then your code changes to

x compareTo: 25
    lesser: [ do something ]
    equals: [ do something else ]
    greater: [ do something entirely different ]

This naturally depends on your particular domain, maybe in different case the wording would be different. E.g. in case of collections, there's col ifEmpty: [ ] ifNotEmpty: [ ], for nil there's ifNil:ifNotNil:, for detection detect:ifFound:ifNone:, for dictionaries at:ifPresent:ifAbsent:, etc.
explanation:
Instead of having a special syntax for control constructs, Pharo offers no special syntax for control constructs. Instead, control constructs are typically expressed by sending messages to booleans, numbers and collections, with blocks as arguments.

Conditionals are expressed by sending one of the messages `ifTrue:`, `ifFalse:`, or `ifTrue:ifFalse:` to the result of a boolean expression. This is because the receiver of these messages is typically a Boolean object, and the method implementations in the Boolean class and its subclasses determine which block to execute based on the receiver.

Loops are typically expressed by sending messages to blocks, integers or collections. For example, the `whileTrue:` message sends a block repeatedly until a certain condition is met.

The `ifTrue:ifFalse:` message is used to define conditional branches in Pharo. It is typically used to execute a block of code if a certain condition is true, and a different block if it is false.

In Pharo, control flow can be expressed naturally using message sends. Seaside, a web development framework built on top of Pharo, makes it particularly easy to design web applications with non-trivial control flow. There are basically two mechanisms that you can use:

1. A component can call another component by sending `caller call: callee`. The caller is temporarily replaced by the callee, until the callee returns control by sending `answer:`.
2. A control flow can also be specified as a task — a instance of a subclass of `WATask`. It should implement the method `go`, which should call: a series of components in a workflow.

In addition, Seaside provides several convenience methods, such as `request:`, `inform:`, `confirm:`, and `chooseFrom:caption:`, for basic interactions with the user.

It's worth noting that Pharo's syntax is minimal. Essentially there is syntax only for sending messages (i.e. expressions). Expressions are built up from a very small number of primitive elements (message sends, assignments, closures, returns...). There are only 6 keywords, and there is no syntax for control structures or declaring new classes. Instead, nearly everything is achieved by sending messages to objects.

title: Usage of self subclassResponsibility
body:
I'm new to Pharo and I'm having trouble grasping some concepts, specifically subclassResponsibilty.

I have the following objecttree:

AbstractDictionary
--TreeBasedDictionary (not abstract)
--AbstractArrayDictionary
----SimpleDictionary (not abstract)
----FastDictionary (not abstract)

All 3 implement the operations at:put: and at: (and more). I understood from class today that I can pull up at:put: to the AbstractDictionary with the following implementation:

at: thisKey put: thisValue
"insert new key / value pair"
^self 
    at: thisKey
    put: thisValue
    duplicate: [self error:'duplicate key']

Then I implemented a metod at:put:duplicate: in TreeBasedDictionary and AbstractArrayDictionary (because the implementation is the same for the latter's subclasses). Then, when I call at:put: on an object it won't find it in the instance of either TreeBasedDictionary , SimpleDictionary or FastDictionary but when looking up the tree it finds this method in the superclass (AbstractDictionary). This will then call self with at:put:duplicate: . This method is implemented by the 3 afore mentioned classes, and then the self which is pointing to the instance of the class that made the initial call is sent the message to execute at:put:duplicate:.

Now, AbstractArrayDictionary,SimpleDictionary and FastDictionary all have a method named size. Both classes at the bottom of the inheritence tree implement this method. AbstractArrayDictionary implements this method as follows

size
"dictionary size"
self subclassResponsibility

Suppose I implemented this method for my TreeBasedDictionary, I suppose I should implement the size method in my AbstractDictionary class as shown above.

My questions: Why do I need to do it like that, and not like this:

size
"dictionary size"
^self size

And if so, do I remove the method from AbstractArrayDictionary and why?
stackoverflow_answer:
Ok, look.

First of all if you use 

size
  ^ self size

you'll end up in the infinite recursion. You ask size of object and the size method asks size of the object itself and so on.

Now in general about what you should know.

  1. You should avoid duplication, that's why you are moving similar methods to the parent nodes in hierarchy. If you have a same method in all subclasses, move it into the superclass without hesitation.
  2. You can just implement methods where they should work. E.i. not write methods with self subclassResponsibility at all. Objects should respond to messages that they can understand. Can AbstractDictionary put elements in it? No. Then there can be no method.
  3. It's not nice to do things that I've written in #2 part. Because
    • when you browse AbstractDictionary class you get an idea that it should support at:put:duplicate:, but the functionality depends on the implementation (aka subclass).
    • when someone will be implementing another subclass of your dictionary, for example MagicBagDictionary and will forget to implement at:put:duplicate:, then during execution he will be gently reminded that this is a subclass responsibility and the method should be implemented. Otherwise he will get an error "message not understood by MagicBagDictionary".

Concerning the size method again: if you know at some point where you can get the size - do it. For example if AbstractDictionary has instance variable container that will hold all the element and should be able to tell the size of them - you can implement

size
  ^ container size

in the AbstractDictionary. But if you have no idea ho the element will be stored and how to calculate their size at that point, you should use:

size
  "dictionary size"
  self subclassResponsibility

This way AbstractDictionary is saying:

yes, you can get my size, but I have no idea how to calculate it because I'm not complete. But my subclasses should know that, so don't hesitate to ask.
explanation:
Based on the provided text chunks, here's a detailed and concise answer to your question:

**Why do I need to do it like that, and not like this:**

In Pharo, methods that send `self subclassResponsibility` are considered abstract by convention. This is because these methods are intended to be implemented by subclasses, rather than being executed directly. When you forget to override an abstract method, an exception is raised.

In your case, you are implementing the `size` method in your `AbstractDictionary` class. Since you are also implementing this method in your `TreeBasedDictionary` class, you should not implement it in the `AbstractDictionary` class. Instead, you should send `self subclassResponsibility` in the `AbstractDictionary` class, indicating that the subclass should implement this method.

This is because the `size` method is intended to be implemented by subclasses of `AbstractDictionary`, such as `TreeBasedDictionary`. By sending `self subclassResponsibility`, you are announcing that the subclass should provide its own implementation of the `size` method.

**When is the pseudovariable self used in Pharo?**

The pseudovariable `self` is used to refer to the receiver of the currently executing method. In other words, `self` always refers to the object that is receiving the message.

**What does Self should do in the context of SUnit testing?**

In SUnit testing, `self` should be used to refer to the test case itself. When writing tests, you can use `self` to refer to the test case, allowing you to access its methods and properties.

**Why are methods that send self subclassResponsibility abstract by convention?**

Methods that send `self subclassResponsibility` are considered abstract by convention because they are intended to be implemented by subclasses, rather than being executed directly. This convention helps to ensure that abstract methods are not executed accidentally, and that subclasses are responsible for implementing them.
