title: Syntax for class and instance variables and methods in Pharo 4.0
body:
I am learning Pharo online and am not sure if I got the syntax correct for creating class and instance variables. Please correct me if I am wrong :-

Class (Static) method created on class side of Pharo, where name, email, phone are instance variables of class CreateUser:

createNewUser:Arguments name:userName email:userEmail phone:userPhone

To call this static method of class CreateUser, I will do the following :-

CreateUser 
     name:userName
     email:userEmail
     phone:userPhone

If I want to create an instance variable by this name, the method declaration will be exactly the same as above, but it will be on the instance side of the class. However, when I call the method, I will call it using the keyword "new" to create a new instance as under:

CreateUser new
     name:userName
     email:userEmail
     phone:userPhone

When I run the above code and call this method statically, I get an error message as:-

MessageNotUnderstood: CreateUser class >>name:email:phone:

However, when I go to the CreateUser class to recheck, I see the above method create on the class side as : 

CreateUser:name:email:phone:

My queries are as below: 1. What am I doing wrong above? How can I fix the above error? 2. The concept behind using static variables/methods vs class variables/methods is the same as Java? 3. If I wish to access the above instance variables, I can add accessor methods for class/instance and then call them with the class instance/class object instance. Is that correct?

Any help you can give will be greatly appreciated! Thanks very much in advance.
stackoverflow_answer:
I guess you misunderstand the method syntax a bit, because createNewUser:Arguments part makes no sense. What you should have is a method on the class side like this:

name: userName email: userEmail phone: userPhone
   "and here you probably have something like:"
   name := userName.
   email := userEmail.
   "and so on"

In my example name:email:phone: is the method's selector and userName, userEmail and userPhone are parameters. You can call this method as in your example. name and email are either class side on instance side variables depending on where the method is defined.

Also you shouldn't name a class CreateUser. Think about this, what will be the instances called? "createUsers"? Usually you name a class User, then you can think about instances as "users", and then the responsibility of the class object is "to create users (its instances)".

Please note, that it's weird to have a method like this on the class side. What you usually do is to create an instance method:

initializeName: userName email: userEmail phone: userPhone
   name := userName.
   email := userEmail.
   phone := userPhone

and a class side method:

newName: userName email: userEmail phone: userPhone
   | instance |
   instance := self new.
   instance initializeName: userName email: userEmail phone: userPhone.
   ^ instance

or a shorter version using cascaded messages:

newName: userName email: userEmail phone: userPhone
   ^ self new
      initializeName: userName email: userEmail phone: userPhone;
      yourself

2) In Pharo (and Smalltalk) the concept is a bit simpler. As everything is an object, Class is an object also, so class side variables and methods are instance variables and methods of a class which is an instance of the "class class". Next picture may help you to understand the associations between objects in Pharo:

This may be a bit confusing at the beginning, but in the end, you don't have and static/nonstatic methods/variables, you just have objects, instantiation and inheritance.

So what you should think about is what are you going to ask the objects about. Probably you should ask User about it's email or mobile number, but you will ask User class to create a user or find a user, or suggest a default t-shirt size for a user.

3) Yes, you should make an accessor. Moreover, if you select a class in system browser and press Cmd+H+A (or Ctrl, or Alt) depending on your OS, you will get a dialog for automatic accessor creation
explanation:
The syntax for class and instance variables and methods in Pharo is as follows:

* Class variables: Class variables are shared between instances and classes. They are defined on the class side and are accessed using the same syntax as instance variables.
* Instance variables: Instance variables are private to the instance and are accessed using the syntax `name:userName` or `phone:userPhone`.
* Class methods: Class methods are defined on the class side and are accessed using the same syntax as instance methods. They are used to provide a way to access class variables and to provide a way to initialize class variables.

The syntax for accessing instance variables is as follows:

* To access an instance variable, you can use the syntax `name:userName` or `phone:userPhone`.
* To access an instance variable from another instance of the same class, you need to define accessor methods on the class side.

The syntax for accessing class variables is as follows:

* To access a class variable, you can use the syntax `classVariableName`.
* Class variables can be accessed from any instance of the class or from the class itself.

The syntax for defining accessor methods is as follows:

* To define an accessor method, you can use the syntax `name:userName`.
* Accessor methods are used to provide a way to access instance variables from another instance of the same class.

The syntax for defining class methods is as follows:

* To define a class method, you can use the syntax `classMethod: methodBody`.
* Class methods are used to provide a way to access class variables and to provide a way to initialize class variables.

In Pharo, the distinction between instance variables and class variables is as follows:

* Instance variables are private to the instance and are accessed using the syntax `name:userName` or `phone:userPhone`.
* Class variables are shared between instances and classes and are accessed using the syntax `classVariableName`.

In terms of the concept behind using static variables/methods vs class variables/methods, Pharo's syntax is as follows:

* Class methods are dynamically-dispatched methods, meaning that inheritance, overriding and super-sends work for class methods in Pharo, whereas they don’t work for static methods in Java.
* Class variables are shared between instances and classes, whereas static variables in Java are shared between classes.

In terms of accessor methods, Pharo's syntax is as follows:

* To access an instance variable from another instance of the same class, you need to define accessor methods on the class side.
* Accessor methods are used to provide a way to access instance variables from another instance of the same class.

In terms of the importance of accessor methods, Pharo's syntax is as follows:

* Accessor methods are used to provide a way to access instance variables from another instance of the same class.
* Accessor methods are important because they provide a way to encapsulate the implementation of instance variables and to provide a way to access them from another instance of the same class.

In terms of the concept behind using class variables, Pharo's syntax is as follows:

* Class variables are shared between instances and classes.
* Class variables can be accessed from any instance of the class or from the class itself.
* Class variables are used to provide a way to share data amongst all the instances of a class and the class itself.

title: If my Pharo image goes into an infinite loop, how do I break out of the loop?
body:
Yesterday I was about 2 hours in to working on my Pharo image when I accidentally wrote some code and executed it that caused an infinite loop -- actually, I was calling one method from another method, which in turn called the original method, back and forth forever. The pharo image was unresponsive. I restarted the image and lost all my work for that two hours!

I looked online and I can hit alt-. to get into some sort of process viewer? How can I kill the infinite loop from there?
stackoverflow_answer:
Apart from interrupting an infinite loop as @Uko explained, you probably do not have lost all your work for that two hours, as you feared.

If you have killed Pharo externally without saving you could always use the

  • „Recover lost changes“ tool in the restarted image via World Menu>Tools>Recover lost changes… or
  • open the .changes file corresponding to your image in a text editor and have a look at all your past changes to the system and copy and paste some or all of them back to the running image.
explanation:
To break out of an infinite loop in Pharo, you can use the `self halt` method. This method will cause the debugger to open, allowing you to examine the state of your program and potentially fix the issue causing the loop.

If you don't want to use `self halt` to set a breakpoint, you can use the `Halt` class to conditionally halt the execution of a method. For example, you can use `Halt now` to halt the execution of a method, or `Halt if: #testOpenInWorld` to halt the execution of a method only if a certain condition is met.

Additionally, you can use the `assert:` message to check for preconditions to public methods of objects. If the assertion fails, the debugger will open, allowing you to examine the state of your program and potentially fix the issue.

There are a few other ways to get into the debugger in Pharo:

* Catch an exception: You can use the `tryCatch` method to catch an exception and enter the debugger.
* Use the `CMD-.` shortcut: You can press `CMD-.` to interrupt a running process and enter the debugger.
* Insert a `Halt now` expression: You can insert a `Halt now` expression in your code to halt the execution of a method and enter the debugger.

The debugger in Pharo is a powerful tool that allows you to execute code step by step, inspect objects in parameters and local variables, evaluate code, and change the code while it is being debugged.

title: creation of a class in Pharo Smalltalk?
body:
I have been following the example of making a class in Pharo from the following link: https://ci.inria.fr/pharo-contribution/job/UpdatedPharoByExample/lastSuccessfulBuild/artifact/book-result/PharoObjectModel/PharoObjectModel.html#fig:colorInstanceClassSide

It is the example of making a Dog and a Hyena class. First I created a package called TestC and in the Instance class I have made the following:

For what I know, and correct me if I am wrong, the instance side is where I create the methods that will work when I instantiate an object while the class side it does not need an object to be created to function; just like a static method class in Java.

The first question that I have at this point is why after Accepting the Changes it stills appear the ! symbol in the left part of my classes?

According to the tutorial then I should put the following code:

Dog class
    instanceVariableNames: 'count'

Now I did not where to put it, on the instance side or in the class side, I decided to put it on the instance side the following:

and the last methods that I have are the following, I put them on the class side:

I have tested the classes in the Transcript with the following code:

aDog := Dog new.
Dog count.
bDog := Dog new.
Dog count.

and it works, but I would like to know if I made the right decision to put those methods in the class side (second question), and if its right could anybody give me an example of a method to put in the instance side in this example?

Thanks
stackoverflow_answer:
First Question

The exclamation mark ! in front of the class is an invitation to click on it. In this case it will remind you that classes are supposed to have comments (for the sake of documentation).

Digression

Every class defines both the shape (or structure) and the behavior of its instances. The shape is given by the instance variables in the class creation message, such as

Object subclass: #Dog
  instanceVariableNames: 'name breed birthdate'
  classVariableNames: ''
  package: 'TestC'

For the behavior you create methods in the instance side of the class

breed: aString
  breed := aString

With this you have a full functional object

dog := Dog new.
dog name: 'Taylor'; breed: 'Great Dane'

However, classes are objects too. So, they may have their own shape. To add an instance variable to a class's shape you send the message:

Dog class instaceVariableNames: 'count'

Note that the receiver of this message is the class of the Dog class (a Metaclass), which makes sense because it is classes who shape their instances.

In your example, every time you create a new (instance of) Dog the count ivar of the Dog class will increase by 1. This could serve the propose of keeping track of how many instances of Dog have been created so far. These instances, however, will remain unaware of this fact.

Second Question

Yes, the ivar count and the methods to initialize, increment and retrieve it belong in the class side. Why? Because they are intended to keep track of the number of instances your class has created. Whether this is useful or not, is a different question; the fact is that they would have made no sense in the instance side (after all, lucky dogs live exciting lives without ever counting anything.)

Pharo is a modern, open-source, dynamically typed language supporting live coding inspired by Smalltalk. It is a lean, open platform for professional software development, as well as a robust and stable platform for research and development into dynamic languages and environments.

**What are several ways to find a class in Pharo?**
There are several ways to find a class in Pharo, including:

1. Knowing or guessing what package it is in, and navigating to it using the browser.
2. Sending the browse message to the class, asking it to open a browser on itself.
3. Using the keyboard shortcut CMD-b (browse) in any editor or browser.
4. Using the History Navigator pulldown, found above the protocol and method panes.
5. Using the Finder tool to search for classes, methods, and pragmas.

**What is required to create a new class or method in the Pharo environment?**
To create a new class or method in the Pharo environment, you will actually have to write some Smalltalk code. You can create a new class by editing the class creation template, replacing Object with the name of the existing class of which you wish to create a subclass, and filling in the instance variable names if you know them.

**What does each class in Pharo inherit?**
Each class in Pharo inherits its behaviour and the description of its structure from a single superclass. This means that Smalltalk has single inheritance.

**Where is new defined?**
When the message new is sent to a class, it is looked up in its metaclass. The metaclass is a class that describes the structure of the class itself, rather than the objects it creates.

title: How can getter/setter code be generated automatically for a class in Pharo or Squeak?
body:
I have a long list of instance variables to create for a class that I want to generate the code for, rather than do it by hand. The list comes from an existing SQL database. My intention is to do it all in a pure object-oriented way with Smalltalk first, and as I learn more, save the data back to the database and work from it directly.

Is there a way of passing the list of names to method that will generate them and add them to the class definition?

In fact is there a way of adding or modifying class definitions dynamically in Smalltalk? I suspect there must and I would like to know a best practices approach.

Update: What I have in mind is more like passing a list of the instance variables to a method that will create them automatically.

It is more like:

addVariablesAndAccessors className: MyClass variablesList: ('aaaa', 'bbbb', 'cccc')

which will then result in a call to

AddVariables className: MyClass variableList: ('aaaa' 'bbbb' cccc')

and

generateAccessors className: MyClass variableList: ('aaaa' 'bbbb' cccc')
stackoverflow_answer:
In Squeak, you have Behavior>>addInstVarName: aString, so for instance, you could do something like:

String addInstVarName: 'foo'

Squeak also has refactoring support to generate accessors automatically. You can either use it directly or have a look at AbstractInstanceVariableRefactoring>>createAccessors to get some inspiration on how to implement your own ;-)

Another quite hacky but not so uncommon solution would be to just generate the instance variables, but instead of adding accessors, you overwrite doesNotUnderstand:, which gets called when an undefined selector is sent to your objects. There, you could check if you have an instance variable named according to the message, and return / change it if it is the case. Otherwise you just do super doesNotUnderstand: aMessage.

Regarding your comment: Classes are objects, too, so you don't have to do anything special to use them as parameters. On which class you add it is totally up to you and doesn't really matter. So a method to add instance variables could look like this:

addVariablesNamed: aCollection on: aClass

    aCollection do: [:each | aClass addInstVarName: each]

and you could call it like this:

yourObject addVariablesNamed: #('foo' 'bar' 'baz') on: ClassX

You can find examples on how to generate accessor methods in the class CreateAccessorsForVariableRefactoring
explanation:
To generate getter/setter code automatically for a class in Pharo, you can use the `DynamicAccessors` class. This class is designed to automatically add accessors for instance variables on demand.

Here is an example of how you can use `DynamicAccessors` to generate getter/setter code:

```smalltalk
myDA := DynamicAccessors new.
myDA x
```

When you send the `x` message to `myDA` for the first time, the `DynamicAccessors` class will generate the accessor methods dynamically and retrieve the value.

Here's a step-by-step explanation of what happens:

1. We send the `x` message to `myDA`.
2. The message is looked up in the class, and since it's not found, the message is not understood.
3. The `doesNotUnderstand` method is called, which looks at the selector of the message (`x`).
4. If an instance variable with the same name as the message exists, the accessor is generated dynamically and the value is retrieved.

This is a powerful feature in Pharo that allows you to dynamically add accessors for instance variables, making your code more readable and maintainable.

Additionally, you can use the `instVarNames` method to check if an instance variable exists, and the `compile` method to generate the accessor code dynamically.

For example:

```smalltalk
| messageName |
messageName := aMessage selector asString.
(self class instVarNames includes: messageName)
    ifTrue: [
        self class compile: messageName, String cr, ' ^ ', messageName.
        ^ aMessage sendTo: self ]
    ^ super doesNotUnderstand: aMessage
```

This code checks if the instance variable exists, and if it does, it generates the accessor code dynamically and sends the message to the object.

Overall, `DynamicAccessors` is a powerful tool in Pharo that allows you to dynamically add accessors for instance variables, making your code more readable and maintainable.

title: Smalltalk - Print something for each 2 seconds
body:
I'm coding in Smalltalk using Pharo. My code:

|i delay|
i := 0.
[i < 5] whileTrue: [
    [
        delay := Delay forSeconds: 2.
        delay wait.
        Transcript show: '2 seconds are up'.
        Transcript cr.
    ] fork.
    i := i + 1.
]

It prints all the "2 seconds are up" at once, and not for each 2 seconds:

2 seconds are up
2 seconds are up
2 seconds are up
2 seconds are up
2 seconds are up

Please, can anyone tell me how to print something for each 2 seconds in Smalltalk?
stackoverflow_answer:
You forked all 5 processes at once so that all 5 delays ends at the same time. To print every 2 seconds from the background, rather fork a whole loop in a separate process:

|i delay|
[   
    i := 0.
    [i < 5] whileTrue: [
        delay := Delay forSeconds: 2.
        delay wait.
        Transcript show: '2 seconds are up'.
        Transcript cr.
        i := i + 1]
] fork

title: Why is Smalltalk sometimes called "dynamically typed"?
body:
I try to understand why Smalltalk is sometimes called "dynamically typed" and not "untyped" which i also read a lot and which seems to make perfect sense.

There are no type annotations for variables, parameters and method returns in Smalltalk - so what would you compare the type of an object (if you say the type of the object is it's class) to?

Also dynamically typed is not the same as dynamically type-checked? So if Smalltalk was dynamically typed it would mean, that internally Smalltalk does give types to variables, parameters, method returns at runtime? does it?
stackoverflow_answer:
"Dynamically typed" is widely used in certain programming language communities to mean "dynamically checked". Likewise, in more theory-oriented circles it is widely regarded as being technically misleading, because what is being checked is not types in any formal sense of the word. However, that usage is far too common these days for any chance of correcting it.

See also my answer to the inverse question for a bit more background.
explanation:
Based on the provided text chunks, Pharo is sometimes called "dynamically typed" because it does not provide type annotations for variables, parameters, and method returns. This means that the type of an object (which is considered to be its class) is not explicitly defined, but rather determined at runtime.

Pharo's syntax is designed to be simple and minimal, with only a few keywords and a focus on sending messages to objects. This syntax is intended to be easy to read and understand, even for those unfamiliar with the language.

In Pharo, everything happens by sending messages to objects. This means that objects are responsible for their own actions, and the method associated with the message is looked up dynamically. When sending a message, the receiver of the message (the object) decides how to respond, rather than the sender.

Pharo's syntax is based on the concept of sending messages to objects. The language does not "invoke methods" but rather sends messages to objects, which then respond accordingly.

The syntax of Pharo is minimal, with only six keywords and no syntax for control structures or declaring new classes. Instead, nearly everything is achieved by sending messages to objects.

Pharo is a reflective language, which means that it is able to reflect on its own execution and structure. This allows for introspection and intercession, which enables the modification of the language semantics and the behavior of a program from within the program itself.

The syntax of Pharo is designed to be simple and easy to read, with a focus on sending messages to objects. The language does not provide type annotations for variables, parameters, and method returns, which is why it is sometimes called "dynamically typed".

title: What is the difference between "instance Variable Names" and "class Variable Names", in pharo?
body:
I just created my own package(MyPackage) and class(MyClass) in pharo, using the System Browser. This is how it looks.

Object subclass: #MyClass
    instanceVariableNames: ''
    classVariableNames: ''
    category: 'MyPackage'

I understand the terms subclass and category, not getting the meaning of the other two terms (instanceVariableNames, classVariableNames).
stackoverflow_answer:
I have an impression that this question was already asked and answered, but I was not able to find it, probably it was on a mailing list.

Instance variables are just the variables that are personal to an instance, so each instance of the class that you are defining will have it's own set of variables.

Instance variables of the class side. You can define that when you switch to the class side, then you will se a code like this:

MyClass class
  instanceVariableNames: ''

In Pharo (and Smalltalks in general) each class is an instance of a meta class. The variables can be accessed by class-side methods, and as there is only a single instance representing class object, there will be only one set of these variables of that instance.

Class variables are the variables defined by classVariableNames: '' on the instance side template. I like to call them "pool variables", but in fact if you define such variable, all the instances from the hierarchy will be able to access it. Let's say that you have a class A and it's subclass B. If you have a "class variable" in A, you can access the same variable from both instances of A and B. It's like having a global variable for a hierarchy. I recommend not to use this type of variables.
explanation:
Here's the combined answer:

**Instance Variables in Pharo:**

* In Pharo, instance variables are private to the instance itself. This means that two instances of the same class cannot access each other's instance variables unless the class defines accessor methods.
* There is no language syntax that provides direct access to the instance variables of any other object. However, a mechanism called reflection does provide a way to ask another object for the values of its instance variables.
* Instance variables can be accessed by name in any of the instance methods of the class that defines them, and also in the methods defined in its subclasses.

**Class Variables in Pharo:**

* Class variables are shared between instances and classes. This means that every class and its instances have access to the same set of class variables.
* Class variables are not to be confused with class instance variables, which are private to the class itself.
* Class variables are used to share data amongst all the instances of a class and the class itself.
* In Pharo, class variables are implemented as a namespace called Smalltalk, which is an instance of the class SystemDictionary.

**Key Differences between Class Instance Variables and Class Methods:**

* Class instance variables and class methods are similar to ordinary instance variables and methods, but they operate on different objects (classes in this case).
* Class instance variables describe the state of classes (instances of metaclasses), while class methods describe the behavior of classes.
* Class instance variables are private to the class itself, while class methods can be accessed by any object that has access to the class.

**Why are Instance Variables Private in Pharo?**

* In Pharo, instance variables are private to the instance itself to maintain encapsulation.
* This means that instance variables are not directly accessible from outside the instance, and methods can only access them through accessor methods.
* This design choice allows for better encapsulation and avoids exposing private state to the world.

**What are Shared Variables in Pharo?**

* Pharo provides three kinds of shared variables: globally shared variables, class variables, and pool variables.
* Global variables are accessible everywhere in the system.
* Class variables are shared between a class, its subclasses, and its instances.
* Pool variables are shared between a selected set of classes.
* Shared variables are used to share data amongst multiple objects.

I hope this combined answer helps clarify the differences between instance and class variables in Pharo!

title: Why do methods return "self" by default in Smalltalk?
body:
Background

In Smalltalk, if you don't excplicitly return anything then the message passing evaluates to the receiver (or "self" in the message context).

For example, given this method:

MyClass >> myMethod
  Transcript show: 'hello'; cr.

Evaluating (doint "print-it") this:

| myInstance |
myInstance := MyClass new.
myInstance myMethod.

If <print-it> is done to the last invocation, then the result would be the instance itself.

Questions

  • Why was this designed this way?
  • What is the idea behind it?
  • What was the philosophical background?
  • What are the practical benefits from it? Is it to facilitate method chaining?
stackoverflow_answer:
One very simple reason has not been stated yet: In the Virtual Machine, returning self is simpler and more efficient than returning any other object.

Smalltalk byte codes implement a stack machine. That means arguments are passed by pushing them onto a stack, rather than putting them into registers. In addition to the arguments as listed in the method signature, a hidden argument is always passed, which is the receiver of the message. So even for unary methods (those without arguments) the receiver is pushed onto the stack, then the method is executed, and the receiver value on the stack is how the method knows "self". By returning "self" if no explicit return statement is given, the VM can just leave the "self" oop on the stack, which saves at least one memory store operation. So from an efficiency and simplicity standpoint, returning "self" is the most elegant thing to do.
explanation:
A method in Pharo always answers a message with a value, which is an object. This value is defined by the `^` construct in the method, but if the method reaches the end without executing a `^`, it still answers the object that received the message. This is known as returning `self`, as `self` represents the receiver of the message.

When a method is called, the virtual machine starts looking for the method in the receiver's class, and then up the inheritance chain. If the method is not found, it sends the message `doesNotUnderstand:` to the receiver, which then looks for the method in its superclass, and so on.

In Pharo, everything happens by sending messages. Even class methods are dynamic, and are looked up in the same way as other methods. This means that class methods can be overridden in subclasses, just like instance methods.

The `^` construct is used to return a value from a method. If a method reaches the end without executing a `^`, it returns `self`, as mentioned earlier.

The uniformity of Pharo's object model means that classes are just regular objects, and behave like any other object. This means that classes can be sent messages, and can respond to those messages by sending messages themselves.

In Pharo, there is no difference between sending a message to an object and sending a message to a class. The method lookup starts in the class of the receiver, and then proceeds up the inheritance chain.

The rules of Pharo's object model are:

1. Everything is an object.
2. Every object is an instance of a class.
3. Every class has a superclass.
4. Everything happens by sending messages.
5. Method lookup follows the inheritance chain.

These rules are applied uniformly throughout the system, which means that there is no magic or special cases.

title: How do we check in pharo if an element already exist in a set
body:
Say I have a function that continually add a random number to a collection after each refresh.

Myfunction
|list length data |

z := Set new.
x := 8.
data:= received data after each refresh.
length := data size.
1 to: length do:[:k | list := data at: k.
    list < x
    "How to check if this data already exist and not add"
        ifTrue:[z add: list]

How can I check in Pharo if a number already exists and if so it should not add it again?
stackoverflow_answer:
Sets and other basic collections are part of common Smalltalk. You should have a look at the Smalltalk documentation (e.g., the Smalltalk bluebook), particularly the references for each of the data types you are attempting to use as well as other related ones.

If you read the documentation for Set you'll find a message #includes: which will return true if the argument is in the set, and return false if it isn't. That would look like:

(z includes: list) ifTrue: [ z add: list ]

But, a Set by definition contains unique elements. Therefore, if you attempt to add an element that's already in the set, Smalltalk will not add it again and not give you an error if you attempt to do so. So you don't need to check to see if the set already includes list. You can just add it.

Then you are doing your data traversal the long, hard way. You don't need to get the length then loop on an index from 1 to that length. I assume your data is some kind of Smalltalk Collection, so you can just use the #do: message. The #do: message allows you to iterate a block over each element of the collection:

data do: [ :each | each < x ifTrue: [ z add: each ] ]

This replaces everything you have shown starting at length := ....
explanation:
To check if an element already exists in a set in Pharo, you can use the `includes:` message. This message is implemented by the `Set` class, which is a collection that behaves as a mathematical set, i.e., a collection with no duplicate elements and without any order. 

Here is an example of how to use the `includes:` message:

```smalltalk
| set |
set := Set new.
set add: 1.
set add: 2.
set add: 3.
set includes: 2
```

In this example, `set` is a set that contains the elements 1, 2, and 3. The `includes:` message is used to check if the element 2 is already in the set. Since 2 is in the set, the `includes:` message will return `true`.

The `includes:` message is used to test if an element is a member of the set. It is often used in combination with other methods, such as `add:` and `remove:`, to manipulate the set.

The `includes:` message is implemented by the `Set` class, which is a subclass of `Collection`, the base class for all collections in Pharo. The `Set` class provides a variety of methods for manipulating sets, including `add:`, `remove:`, `size`, `isEmpty`, and `includes:`.

In addition to `includes:`, the `Set` class also provides the `add:`, `remove:`, `size`, and `isEmpty` messages. The `add:` message is used to add an element to the set, the `remove:` message is used to remove an element from the set, the `size` message is used to get the number of elements in the set, and the `isEmpty` message is used to test if the set is empty.

Here is an example of how to use the `add:` and `remove:` messages:

```smalltalk
| set |
set := Set new.
set add: 1.
set add: 2.
set add: 3.
set add: 2.
set size
set remove: 2
set size
```

In this example, `set` is a set that contains the elements 1, 2, and 3. The `add:` message is used to add the elements to the set. The `remove:` message is used to remove the element 2 from the set. The `size` message is used to get the number of elements in the set before and after the element is removed.

title: How to implement a switch in Pharo Smalltalk
body:
I'm trying to parse a command and an int to make a "turtle" move on a board. I'm a bit overwhelmed, since it's not throwing an exception, and I can't even figure out how to open the debugger without one.

My code:

"only should begin parsing on new line"
endsWithNewLine:= aTurtleProgram endsWith: String cr.
endsWithNewLine ifTrue:[
    "splits commands based on new lines"
    commands := aTurtleProgram splitOn: String cr.
    commands do:[:com |
        "should split into command and value for command"
        i := com splitOn: ' '.
        "command"
        bo := ci at: 1.
        val := ci at: 2.
        "value for command"
        valInt := val asInteger.
        ^ bo = 'down' "attempted switch"
            ifTrue: [ turtle down: valInt ]
            ifFalse: [
              bo = 'left'
                  ifTrue: [ turtle left: valInt ]
                  ifFalse: [
                    bo = 'right'
                        ifTrue: [ turtle right: valInt ]
                        ifFalse: [
                          bo = 'up'
                              ifTrue: [ turtle up: valInt ]
                              ifFalse: [ self assert: false ] ] ] ] ].
stackoverflow_answer:
You can do it the way you did it, and you can open a debugger by inserting a self halt statement into your code.

Usually, ifs, and moreover case-sytle ifs, are a bad sign. So what you can do is break the functionality into classes like DownMove, LeftMove and so on, and then each class will implement its own functionality when you call, for example, an execute: method that will do exactly what is needed by the command. But in your case, the approach would be cumbersome; moreover, you have very trivial actions.

You can use a dictionary with definitions. So imagine that you define an instance variable or class variable:

moveActions := {
  'down' -> [ :dist | turtle down: dist ] .
  'left' -> [ :dist | turtle left: dist ] .
  ... } asDictionary

Then in your code, you do: (moveActions at: bo) value: valInt. This snippet will give you a block (value) for a string (key), then you evaluate the block with your integer.

On the other hand, as the action pattern is the same and only message changes, you can map only the message names in the dictionary:

moveActions := {
  'down' -> #down: .
  'left' -> #left: .
  ... } asDictionary

Then you can ask your turtle to perform a message dynamically given by string: 

`turtle perform: (moveActions at: bo) with: valInt`

Also, if you want to rely on the similarity between commands that you read and messages that you send to the turtle, you can dynamically compose the message string:

`turtle perform: (bo, ':') asSymbol with: valInt`

Please note that this is not recommended in your case, as, first of all, you are coupling user input and your code, i.e. if you decide to change the user command from down to moveDown, you will have to change your method name from down: to moveDown:. Also, this approach allows the user to "inject" bad code into your system, as he can write a command like become 42, which will result in the code:

`turtle perform: #become: with: 42`

which will swap pointers between the turtle object and 42. Or you can think about even worse cases. But I hope that this meta excursion was good for you. :)
explanation:
The question is how to implement a switch in Pharo Smalltalk. A switch statement is a control flow statement that allows you to execute a different block of code based on the value of a variable or expression. In Pharo, you can implement a switch statement using a combination of conditionals and blocks.

Here's an example of how you can implement a switch statement in Pharo:
```
| value |
value := 3.  // or some other value

value ifTrue: [ 
    "execute code for true value"
] ifFalse: [ 
    "execute code for false value"
].
```
In this example, the `ifTrue:` and `ifFalse:` messages are sent to the value object. The `ifTrue:` message is sent when the value is true, and the `ifFalse:` message is sent when the value is false.

You can also use the `ifNil:` and `ifNotNil:` messages to implement a switch statement. These messages are sent to the value object, and they execute the corresponding block of code based on whether the value is nil or not.

For example:
```
| value |
value := nil.  // or some other value

value ifNil: [ 
    "execute code for nil value"
] ifNotNil: [ 
    "execute code for non-nil value"
].
```
In this example, the `ifNil:` message is sent when the value is nil, and the `ifNotNil:` message is sent when the value is not nil.

You can also use the `caseOf:` message to implement a switch statement. This message is sent to the value object, and it executes the corresponding block of code based on the value.

For example:
```
| value |
value := 3.  // or some other value

value caseOf: [
    1 -> [ 
        "execute code for value 1"
    ]
    2 -> [ 
        "execute code for value 2"
    ]
    default -> [ 
        "execute code for default value"
    ]
].
```
In this example, the `caseOf:` message is sent to the value object, and it executes the corresponding block of code based on the value.

It's worth noting that Pharo's switch statement is more flexible than traditional switch statements in other languages. It allows you to execute different blocks of code based on different values, and it also allows you to execute code based on whether the value is nil or not.

title: In Pharo/Smalltalk: How to read a file with a specific encoding?
body:
I am currently reading a file like this:

dir := FileSystem disk workingDirectory.
stream := (dir / 'test.txt' ) readStream.
line := stream nextLine.

This works when the file is utf-8 encoded but I could not find out what to do when the file has another encoding.
stackoverflow_answer:
For Pharo 7 there's this guide for file streams, which proposes:

('test.txt' asFileReference)
    readStreamEncoded: 'cp-1250' do: [ :stream |
        stream upToEnd ].
explanation:
To read a file with a specific encoding in Pharo/Smalltalk, you can use the `FileStream` class. Here's an example:

```smalltalk
FileStream forceNewFileNamed: 'test.txt' do: [:stream |
    stream
        binary;  "Specify the encoding"
        nextPutAll: '123';
        cr;
        nextPutAll: 'abcd' ].
```

In this example, we create a new file named `test.txt` and write the strings `'123'` and `'abcd'` to it. The `binary` message is used to specify that the file should be written in binary format, which is useful when working with files that contain non-ASCII characters.

To read a file with a specific encoding, you can use the `readFileNamed:` method of the `FileStream` class. For example:

```smalltalk
FileStream readOnlyFileNamed: 'test.txt' do: [:stream |
    stream contents.
    "Read the contents of the file"
    "Process the contents as needed"
].
```

In this example, we open the file `test.txt` for reading and read its contents. You can then process the contents of the file as needed.

Regarding regular expressions in Pharo, the language supports regular expressions through the `Regex` package, which is installed by default. You can use regular expressions to match patterns in strings, extract subexpressions, and replace or translate subexpressions in a new copy of the string.

Here's an example of how you can use regular expressions in Pharo:

```smalltalk
| re |
re := '([CARETaeiou]+)([aeiou]+)' asRegex.
re matchesPrefix: 'pharo'
>>> true
re subexpression: 1
>>> 'pha'
re subexpression: 2
>>> 'ph'
re subexpression: 3
>>> 'a'
```

In this example, we define a regular expression that matches one or more vowels followed by one or more consonants. We then use the `matchesPrefix:` message to see if the regular expression matches the string `'pharo'`. The `subexpression:` message is used to extract the subexpressions of the regular expression.

As for how to save plain code in Pharo, the simplest way is by "filing out" the code. You can right-click on the package in the Package pane and select "File Out" to save the code as a file. The resulting file is more or less human-readable and is intended for computers, not humans. You can email this file to your friends, and they can file it into their own Pharo images using the file list browser.

It's worth noting that Pharo also supports versioning tools like Monticello, which offer much better ways to manage versions and share code amongst developers.

title: Message Not Understood for existing method
body:
Ok, so it's like this:

receiver selectors

returns

#(#expression #expression: #mondrianLabel #accept: #name)

But 

receiver expression

throws MessageNotUnderstood.

Any ides how to fix this and why this can be caused?
stackoverflow_answer:
You are probably sending the message expression to a class instead of an instance of that class.

If the result of evaluating receiver selectors is #(#expression #expression: #mondrianLabel #accept: #name) then receiver is most probably a class. The message #selectors is implemented on the class Behavior and returns the selectors of the instance methods of the class. In other words: instances of the class (or any of its subclasses) understand the messages listed.

Hence, if you evaluate receiver expression, then you will get a message not understood exception because the message #expression is defined on the instance of the class that is the value of your receiver variable.
explanation:
The `doesNotUnderstand:` method is executed when an object does not understand a message. This method is a part of the Object protocol and is responsible for handling messages that are not understood by an object. When an object receives a message that it does not understand, it sends itself the `doesNotUnderstand:` message with the message selector as its argument. This process is repeated until the `doesNotUnderstand:` method is found, at which point the debugger is launched.

The `doesNotUnderstand:` method is a convenient way to intercept messages that are not understood by an object. This method can be overridden by subclasses of Object to provide alternative behavior for handling messages that are not understood. Two common applications of this technique are to implement lightweight proxies for objects and to dynamically compile or load missing code.

In Pharo, when an object receives a message, it first looks in the method dictionary of its class for a corresponding method to respond to the message. If no such method exists, it will continue looking up the class hierarchy, until it reaches Object. If still no method is found for that message, the object will send itself the `doesNotUnderstand:` message with the message selector as its argument. The process then starts all over again, until the `doesNotUnderstand:` method is found, and the debugger is launched.

The `doesNotUnderstand:` method is commonly abbreviated as DNU or MNU and is sent whenever message lookup fails. The default implementation of this method is to trigger the debugger at this point. It may be useful to override `doesNotUnderstand:` to provide some other behavior.

In Pharo, messages are understood as follows:

* Unary messages are sent first.
* Binary messages are sent next.
* Keyword messages are sent last.

The order in which messages are sent is determined by the type of message. There are just three types of messages: unary, binary, and keyword messages.

When an object receives a message, it first looks in the method dictionary of its class for a corresponding method to respond to the message. If no such method exists, it will continue looking up the class hierarchy, until it reaches Object. If still no method is found for that message, the object will send itself the `doesNotUnderstand:` message with the message selector as its argument.

References:
* "Pharo By Example" textbook, pages 107, 312-313, 322, 187, 71-72, 85-87, 101.

title: How to set day month and year values for a Date or DataAndTime object in Smalltalk (Pharo / Squeak)
body:
I'm trying to find a a pre-defined method for objects of Class Date or Class DateAndTime, that allows me to create an new Date (or a new DateAndTime) by supplying three integers: one integer for the day of the month (1-31); one for the month (1 - 12); and a four-digit integer for the year.

( The closest I've found so far is Integer>>asYear )

Is there a method that can set all three parameters at once?
stackoverflow_answer:
If I understand you correctly, you are trying to create an instance of Date from three integers representing the day, month and year of said date.

When facing a question like this you can browse the class, Date in this case, and check its protocol for instance creation methods. In Pharo there are several methods in this category, but it is easy so see (I think) that #year:month:day looks like a good candidate. So, you can try it. Just evaluate the expression

Date year: 2015 month: 12 day: 31

and see what happens (you can inspect or print it to see the result).

You will also find #newDay:month:year as another good candidate. You could try it too. Or you could just see that it sends our previous message and thus it is just a synonymous (which is present for compatibility with other dialects that support the Smalltalk-80/ANSI specification).
explanation:
Based on the provided text chunks, here are the answers to the questions:

**How to set day, month, and year values for a Date or DateAndTime object in Smalltalk (Pharo / Squeak)?**

There is no direct method to set all three parameters at once. However, you can create a new Date or DateAndTime object using the `asDate` or `asDateAndTime` methods, respectively. For example:
```smalltalk
| date |
date := 'February 29, 2004' asDate.
```

The most generic ways to create instances of collections in Smalltalk (Pharo) are:

* `new: anInteger` creates a collection of size `anInteger` whose elements will all be `nil`.
* `with: anObject` creates a collection and adds `anObject` to the created collection.
* `(p. 201)` - You can create collections with initial elements using the methods `with:`, `with:with:`, etc. for up to six elements.

* Execute `Smalltalk globals at: #AGlobalName put: nil` to define a global programmatically. To remove it, execute `Smalltalk globals removeKey: #AGlobalName`.

**Which data values are Numbers in Pharo?**

In Pharo, numbers are not primitive data values but true objects. The abstract root of the number hierarchy is Magnitude, which represents all kinds of classes supporting comparison operators. The Number hierarchy includes classes such as Float, Fraction, and SmallInteger, which support various arithmetic and other operators.

**How to create instances of the class Array?**

You can create instances of the class Array using the following methods:

* `new: anInteger` creates an array of size `anInteger`. For example: `Array new: 5`.
* `with: anObject` creates an array and adds `anObject` to the created array. For example: `Array with: 1`.

title: How to sort a Dictionary by values in Smalltalk?
body:
I've got a Dictionary like this:

a PluggableDictionary(
    Rankable1->8.5
    Rankable2->9.0
)

I need just an OrderedCollection with the Rankable objects in descending order:

a OrderedCollection(
    Rankable2
    Rankable1
)

I noticed it is easy to sort by keys, but I found it a bit more difficult to sort by values. What is the smalltalk way of doing this?
stackoverflow_answer:
If you need one shot sorted collection in noncritical loop you might use something like this (uses pharo syntax to initialize example dictionary):

pd := PluggableDictionary newFromPairs: { 'a' . 2 . 'b' . 1 . 'c' . 3} . 

(pd associations asSortedCollection: [:x :y | x value < y value]) 
            collect: [:assoc | assoc key].

If you would need it more often, than you might consider introducing your own class that will keep this collection calculated.
explanation:
To sort a Dictionary by values in Smalltalk, you can use the following approach:

1. Convert the Dictionary into an OrderedCollection using the `withAll:` message.

Example:
```smalltalk
aPluggableDictionary := PluggableDictionary newFrom: {
    Rankable1 -> 8.5.
    Rankable2 -> 9.0
}.
orderedCollection := aPluggableDictionary withAll.
```

2. Then, you can sort the OrderedCollection using the `asSortedCollection` message.

Example:
```smalltalk
sortedCollection := orderedCollection asSortedCollection.
```

This will give you an OrderedCollection with the Rankable objects in descending order.

Here's the complete example:
```smalltalk
aPluggableDictionary := PluggableDictionary newFrom: {
    Rankable1 -> 8.5.
    Rankable2 -> 9.0
}.
orderedCollection := aPluggableDictionary withAll.
sortedCollection := orderedCollection asSortedCollection.
```

According to the Pharo documentation, the `withAll:` message creates a new instance of the receiving class, and then adds all elements of the given collection to this new instance.

In this case, `aPluggableDictionary withAll` creates a new instance of `OrderedCollection` and adds all the Rankable objects to it. Then, `asSortedCollection` sorts the OrderedCollection in ascending order based on the values of the Rankable objects.

You can also use the `valuesDo:` message to iterate over the values of the Dictionary, and then sort them in ascending order.

Example:
```smalltalk
aPluggableDictionary := PluggableDictionary newFrom: {
    Rankable1 -> 8.5.
    Rankable2 -> 9.0
}.
values := aPluggableDictionary valuesDo: [:value | value].
sortedValues := values asSortedCollection.
```

This will give you an OrderedCollection with the values of the Rankable objects in ascending order.

Note that the `asSortedCollection` message sorts the collection based on the natural order of the elements. In this case, the natural order is the ascending order of the values.

title: How to check if two arrays contain the same elements?
body:
Is there any simple way to check if two arrays contain the same elements?

Here is my try were I return true if arrays are not the same:

arr1 := #(1 3 5 6).
arr2 := #(1 2 3 4).
arr2Copy := arr2 copyFrom: 1 to: arr2 size.
arr1 size ~= arr2 size
    ifTrue: [^ true].
arr1
    do: [:a | (arr2copy removeFirst = a)
        ifFalse: [^ true]].
^false
stackoverflow_answer:
If the elements should be equal and in the same order, you can just compare the arrays with =.

If the elements should be equal and the order does not matter and there are no duplicates to be expected, use array1 asSet = arr2 asSet.

Otherwise you can check out hasEqualElements:, and asBag as well.

If the elements should be identical and in the same order, how about this?

array1 with: array2 do:
    [ :a :b | a == b ifFalse: [ ^ false ]].
 ^ true

It iterates over the two arrays simultaneously, comparing the identities of elements at the same indices. If any are not identical, return false. If no distinct elements were encountered, return true.
explanation:
To check if two arrays contain the same elements, you can redefine the equality method (=) in the Array class. This method should compare the elements of the two arrays, not their identities. 

In Pharo, the default implementation of object equality is to test for object identity. However, in some cases, you might want to override this method to test for object equality instead. For example, you might want to consider two complex numbers equal if they have the same real and imaginary parts, even if they are different objects.

Here is an example of how you can redefine the equality method for arrays:

```smalltalk
Array >> = anArray
    ^ self size = anArray size
        and: [self at:1 to: self size do: [:e | anArray at:1 to: anArray size detect: [:a | a = e] thenNot: [^ false]]]]
```

This method checks if the two arrays have the same size, and then checks if the elements at the same indices in the two arrays are equal. If both conditions are true, it returns true, indicating that the two arrays contain the same elements.

You can also use the following method to compare the contents of two arrays:

```smalltalk
Array >> sameContents: anArray
    ^ self size = anArray size
        and: [self at:1 to: self size do: [:e | (anArray at:1 to: anArray size detect: [:a | a = e] then: [^ false])]]]
```

This method is similar to the previous one, but it returns a boolean value indicating whether the two arrays contain the same elements.
